overview: SPIRAL is a self-iterating autonomous development loop (Research â†’ Implement â†’ Validate). This PRD drives Spiral to improve itself: integrating newly-written lib modules into the main loop, enhancing the setup wizard to educate users about every configurable option, and adding Chrome DevTools-powered visual validation.
goals: Wire the four new lib modules (preflight, DAG, state machine, story review) into the live spiral.sh loop
Enhance /spiral-init wizard to ask about ALL configurable options, educating users about what exists
Achieve dynamic token-saving without sacrificing output quality via smarter model routing
Enable Chrome DevTools MCP as a first-class visual validation tool in Phase V

userStories.title: Detect and reset dirty worker worktrees at startup to recover from abrupt session termination
userStories.description: When Claude Code sessions stop abruptly (OOM kill, user interrupt, network drop), worker worktrees are left with staged/unstaged changes and reported as dirty submodules. On spiral.sh startup (before launching workers), detect each worker worktree with non-empty git status --porcelain output. For each dirty worktree, run git reset HEAD followed by git checkout -- . to restore a clean state. Emit a worker_reset_dirty_worktree event to spiral_events.jsonl listing which worktrees were cleaned. This ensures the next spiral run starts from a consistent state without manual intervention.

userStories.title: Auto-cleanup stale spiral-worker-* branches on startup
userStories.description: Crashed runs leave behind orphaned spiral-worker-* branches. At startup of run_parallel_ralph.sh, scan for and delete any spiral-worker-* branches not checked out in any live worktree, then run worktree prune. Prevents branch accumulation that degrades git performance.

userStories.title: Remove orphaned index.lock files before worktree creation and in cleanup
userStories.description: OOM-killed workers leave .git/index.lock behind, causing all subsequent git operations to silently fail. In the Step 2 worktree creation loop and in cleanup_parallel(), detect and remove index.lock files from previously crashed workers before attempting new git operations.

userStories.title: Auto-restore prd.json from backup when corruption detected at iteration start
userStories.description: Validate prd.json with `jq empty` at the start of each iteration. If corrupted by a mid-write crash, restore from the most recent prd-backups/prd-iter*.json snapshot. Also fix the two non-atomic direct writes to prd.json in sequential mode to use temp+mv.

userStories.title: Add configurable timeout for Phase T (synthesize_tests.py) with SPIRAL_TEST_SYNTH_TIMEOUT
userStories.description: Phase T had no timeout and could hang indefinitely blocking the iteration. Wrap synthesize_tests.py and spiral-core synthesize in timeout --kill-after=30. Exit code 124 (timeout) produces a warning event and safe empty output. Adds SPIRAL_TEST_SYNTH_TIMEOUT env var (default 60s).

userStories.title: Validate heartbeat requeue succeeded and force-reset on failure
userStories.description: requeue_stale_stories() was called with || true — if it silently failed the story stayed stuck forever. After calling it, verify the story passes field is now false. If not, force-reset it via direct jq write with a warning log.

userStories.title: Detect and log .rej files after git apply --reject fallback
userStories.description: When git apply --3way unexpectedly fails and falls back to --reject, .rej files silently mark broken state. After the --reject apply, scan for new *.rej files, log them with a manual-review warning, and emit a patch_rejected event to spiral_events.jsonl.

userStories.title: Add predictive preemptive memory pressure trigger in memory-watchdog.ps1
userStories.description: The watchdog was purely reactive. Add -PreemptivePressureMB parameter: when free RAM drops below threshold at level 0, pre-escalate to level 1 proactively before hitting hard thresholds. Controlled by SPIRAL_PREEMPTIVE_PRESSURE_MB (default 0 = disabled). Passed through from spiral.sh watchdog invocation.

userStories.title: Detect and alert when deferred worker launch queue is stalled
userStories.description: When all queued workers wait indefinitely due to sustained memory pressure, only the periodic Queue: N deferred message was emitted with no escalation. Track _QUEUE_STALL_SECS across loop cycles. After SPIRAL_QUEUE_STALL_WARN_SECS (default 600s), emit a prominent warning suggesting reduce RALPH_WORKERS or lower SPIRAL_WORKER_MEMORY_LIMIT.

userStories.title: Merge per-worker retry-counts.json back into root after parallel run
userStories.description: Per-worker retry-counts.json files were never merged back to root after parallel runs. After merge_worker_results.py, collect all worker retry-counts.json files and merge into the root file using max-value semantics — ensuring retry counts are never decremented across parallel runs.

userStories.title: Clean up stale /tmp spiral files from previous crashed runs on startup
userStories.description: ralph-bench-output-worker-*.txt files and spiral-docker-lock-* dirs accumulate in /tmp across runs and are never cleaned. At startup of run_parallel_ralph.sh, remove all Spiral-specific /tmp files from previous invocations (safe: patterns are SPIRAL-specific and never needed across runs).

userStories.title: Add SIGINT/SIGTERM trap to spiral.sh for graceful shutdown and checkpoint flush
userStories.description: spiral.sh uses set -euo pipefail but has no signal trap. A Ctrl+C mid-iteration leaves background ralph workers running as orphan processes, may leave prd.json partially written by jq, and does not write a clean checkpoint. Adding a trap handler that kills child processes, flushes the current phase to checkpoint, and prints a summary message before exit allows the user to resume cleanly.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral.sh registers signal handlers: trap '_spiral_cleanup INT' INT and trap '_spiral_cleanup TERM' TERM near the top after variable initialization

_spiral_cleanup kills the current background child process group, writes {"iter": N, "phase": P} to _checkpoint.json atomically, and prints '[SPIRAL] Interrupted at iter N phase P â€” run again to resume'
After Ctrl+C mid-Phase-I: no orphan ralph.sh or claude CLI processes remain (verified with ps aux | grep ralph)
Rerunning spiral.sh after interrupt resumes from the correct phase per checkpoint
uv run pytest passes (51/51)
technicalNotes: Track child PIDs: CHILD_PIDS=(); add each background PID via CHILD_PIDS+=($!)
Cleanup: for pid in "${CHILD_PIDS[@]:-}"; do kill -TERM "$pid" 2>/dev/null || true; done
Checkpoint flush inside handler: echo "{\"iter\": $ITER, \"phase\": $PHASE}" > $_checkpoint_tmp && mv $_checkpoint_tmp _checkpoint.json
Note: set -e in cleanup handlers can cause issues â€” use explicit || true

userStories.title: Move retry-count increment responsibility from spiral.sh to ralph.sh for correctness
userStories.description: spiral.sh currently increments retry counts in retry-counts.json after ralph.sh returns. If ralph is killed by a timeout, OOM, or SIGTERM before completing, the outer loop never runs its increment code and the retry count stays at 0, allowing a pathological story to consume infinite timeout-killed attempts. Moving the increment into ralph.sh itself (on any non-clean exit) makes retry counting immune to outer-loop failures.

technicalNotes: Capture claude stderr to a temp file: claude ... 2>"$RALPH_STDERR_TMP"

A SPIRAL_COST_CEILING config (in USD) reads from results.tsv to estimate cumulative token spend using Anthropic pricing and aborts before the next iteration if the ceiling is reached, preventing runaway costs.

At the start of each iteration, spiral.sh computes cumulative estimated USD cost from results.tsv using haiku/sonnet/opus per-token pricing
If cumulative cost >= SPIRAL_COST_CEILING, spiral.sh prints a cost summary and exits with exit code 2
Estimated cumulative cost is printed in the per-iteration summary
Unit test: mock results.tsv with known token counts and assert cost computation matches expected USD
technicalNotes: Anthropic pricing (2025): haiku ~$0.80/$4 per MTok in/out, sonnet ~$3/$15, opus ~$15/$75
Store pricing in a PRICING_TABLE bash associative array or lib/pricing.py
results.tsv columns include model; token counts can be approximated from output size if not directly available

userStories.title: Write structured JSONL event log to .spiral/spiral_events.jsonl
userStories.description: Spiral has no structured observability layer beyond progress.txt prose and results.tsv. A JSONL event log at .spiral/spiral_events.jsonl capturing every phase transition, story state change, and fatal error enables post-run debugging and feeds future analytics tooling.
acceptanceCriteria: Every phase transition in spiral.sh appends a JSON event: {ts, event:'phase_start'|'phase_end', phase, iteration}
Every story completion in ralph.sh (pass or fail) appends: {ts, event:'story_passed'|'story_failed', storyId, retryCount, model}
Every fatal error appends: {ts, event:'error', message, context}
.spiral/spiral_events.jsonl is valid JSONL (one JSON object per line) after a full run
Log appends across restarts â€” it is not truncated on resume
technicalNotes: Implement log_event() helper in spiral.sh using printf + jq for JSON escaping
Use ISO-8601 timestamps: date --iso-8601=seconds (GNU) or date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ
Keep writes non-blocking using >> (append) not atomic write
Rotate when exceeding SPIRAL_EVENT_LOG_MAX_LINES (default: 10000)

userStories.title: Cache research HTTP responses in .spiral/research_cache/ with TTL
userStories.description: Phase R re-fetches the same documentation URLs on every Spiral iteration, burning Firecrawl credits and slowing research. Caching responses by URL hash with a configurable TTL (default 24h) eliminates redundant fetches when iterating on the same project across multiple runs.
acceptanceCriteria: A SPIRAL_RESEARCH_CACHE_TTL_HOURS config variable (default: 24) controls cache lifetime
When the research agent fetches a URL, the response is saved to .spiral/research_cache/<md5-of-url>.json with a fetched_ts field
On subsequent fetches of the same URL within TTL, the cached response is returned without an HTTP call
Setting SPIRAL_RESEARCH_CACHE_TTL_HOURS=0 disables the cache entirely
Cache entries older than TTL are automatically pruned at the start of Phase R
technicalNotes: Cache key = md5(normalized_url); store as JSON: {url, fetched_ts, content}
Implement as a shell wrapper or inject cache-check instructions into the Phase R research prompt
The .gitignore should exclude .spiral/research_cache/ by default

userStories.title: Record phase start/end timestamps in .spiral/_checkpoint.json
userStories.description: The checkpoint stores iter and phase but not timing data, making it impossible to identify bottleneck phases across iterations. Adding phaseDurations (phase -> elapsed_seconds) to the checkpoint enables performance analysis and informs capacity planning for long-running projects.
acceptanceCriteria: _checkpoint.json gains a phaseDurations field: {R: N, T: N, M: N, I: N, V: N} in integer seconds
Each phase records its elapsed time before writing the checkpoint
state_machine.py validate-phases accepts and validates phaseDurations as optional positive numbers
Dashboard or per-iteration summary log shows per-phase timing for the last completed iteration
technicalNotes: Use $SECONDS bash builtin or PHASE_START=$(date +%s); ELAPSED=$(( $(date +%s) - PHASE_START ))
Phase I (ralph) timing should include total time across all worker processes
Store durations as integers (whole seconds)

userStories.title: Record _passedCommit git SHA in prd.json when story passes
userStories.description: When ralph marks a story passes=true, there is no link back to the git commit that implemented it. Adding a _passedCommit field with the HEAD SHA provides complete traceability: given any story ID you can run git show <SHA> to see exactly what code change satisfied it.
acceptanceCriteria: After ralph commits changes and before writing passes=true, it reads git rev-parse HEAD and stores it in story._passedCommit
prd_schema.py accepts _passedCommit as an optional string field without emitting errors
If git rev-parse fails (no git history), _passedCommit is omitted rather than erroring
story_review_report.py includes _passedCommit as a hyperlink column
technicalNotes: In ralph.sh: COMMIT_SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD 2>/dev/null || echo '')
Use jq to inject: jq '(.userStories[] | select(.id == "$STORY_ID")) += {_passedCommit: "$COMMIT_SHA"}' prd.json
prd_schema.py: optional validation â€” _passedCommit must match /^[0-9a-f]{40}$/ or be empty string

userStories.title: Add epicId grouping field to PRD schema and story review report
userStories.description: Large PRDs have dozens of stories with no grouping mechanism. An optional epicId field allows grouping related stories into milestones (e.g., 'auth', 'dashboard', 'api'). The story review report and dashboard should render stories grouped by epic with per-epic completion progress.
acceptanceCriteria: prd_schema.py accepts an optional epicId string field on each story; validates it is a non-empty string if present
story_review_report.py groups stories by epicId in HTML output, with a per-epic progress bar

technicalNotes: epicId is a free-form string like 'auth', 'payments', 'onboarding'
Optional prd.json top-level epics array: [{id, title, description}] for epic metadata

userStories.title: Add SPIRAL_ON_COMPLETE hook variable for post-completion automation
userStories.description: When Spiral finishes all stories, there is no way to trigger downstream automation without modifying spiral.sh. A SPIRAL_ON_COMPLETE environment variable holds a shell command executed once when the loop completes successfully, enabling webhooks, desktop notifications, CI triggers, and Slack alerts.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral.config.sh template documents SPIRAL_ON_COMPLETE with a commented example
When SPIRAL_ON_COMPLETE is set and check_done exits 0, spiral.sh runs eval "$SPIRAL_ON_COMPLETE" before exiting

userStories.title: Add unit tests for synthesize_tests.py (report parsing and priority mapping)
userStories.description: synthesize_tests.py is a Phase T critical-path component with complex logic: finding recent reports, normalizing test names, deduplicating against PRD stories, and mapping categories to priorities. It has no unit tests, making refactoring risky.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/test_synthesize_tests.py covers find_recent_reports with missing dir, empty dir, and multi-report scenarios
Tests cover normalize() with punctuation, unicode, and empty string inputs
Tests cover PRIORITY_MAP for all known categories and unknown-category fallback to 'medium'

technicalNotes: Use tmp_path pytest fixture for file-system based tests

Import path: sys.path.insert(0, 'lib') then from synthesize_tests import ...

userStories.title: Add unit tests for merge_stories.py (deduplication and atomic prd.json patch)
userStories.description: merge_stories.py orchestrates Phase M, patching prd.json with new research stories. Its word-overlap deduplication, ID assignment, priority ordering, and overflow logic are untested. A bug could silently insert duplicate stories or corrupt prd.json.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/test_merge_stories.py covers overlap_ratio with identical, partial, and disjoint title pairs

Test priority ordering: critical stories appear before medium stories in merged output
Test the --max-new flag at boundary values (0, 1, N)

userStories.title: Add unit tests for state_machine.py (StoryLifecycle and phase transitions)
userStories.description: state_machine.py contains the SpiralPhaseStateMachine and StoryLifecycle classes enforcing correctness invariants. It has doctest examples but no pytest suite. Without systematic tests, an inadvertent relaxation of transition rules could allow corrupted states to pass validation silently.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/test_state_machine.py covers all valid StoryLifecycle transitions: pending->implementing->passed, failed_retry->implementing->passed
Tests cover all invalid transitions (e.g., passed->implementing) and verify InvalidTransition is raised

Test new_iteration() resets phase ordering correctly
Test validate_story_states() with a PRD where a passed story has an unresolved dependency

userStories.title: Implement worker heartbeat file for stale-worker detection and auto-requeue
userStories.description: A crashed parallel ralph worker (OOM kill, API hang) leaves its assigned story stuck indefinitely. Each worker should write a heartbeat timestamp every 30 seconds; the coordinator detects stale heartbeats (>120s) and re-queues the story without incrementing retryCount.
acceptanceCriteria: Each parallel worker writes .spiral/workers/<worker_id>.heartbeat containing {pid, storyId, ts} every 30 seconds
The coordinator checks heartbeat files every 60 seconds; a file not updated in >120s is considered stale
When stale, the story is re-queued (passes stays false, retryCount NOT incremented) and a warning is logged
Heartbeat files are cleaned up on normal worker exit
Integration test: kill a worker mid-run and verify the story is re-queued within 3 minutes
technicalNotes: bash: while sleep 30; do echo '{...}' > .spiral/workers/$WORKER_ID.heartbeat; done & (background loop)
Coordinator: find .spiral/workers/ -name '*.heartbeat' -mmin +2 | while read f; do requeue; done
On Windows use a PowerShell Start-Job for the heartbeat background loop

userStories.title: Add schemaVersion field to prd.json with auto-migration on load
userStories.description: prd.json has no version field, making it impossible to detect schema changes and safely migrate old files. Projects started months ago on an older Spiral version may have structurally incompatible prd.json files. A schemaVersion field enables migration scripts and clear error messages.
acceptanceCriteria: prd_schema.py accepts an optional schemaVersion integer field (current version: 1)
prd_schema.py emits a warning (not error) when schemaVersion is absent, suggesting the user run spiral --migrate
A lib/migrate_prd.py script upgrades prd.json from any prior version to the current schema (adds missing required fields with defaults)
spiral.sh runs a migration check at startup and aborts with a clear message if prd.json is on an incompatible future version
New prd.json files created by spiral-init include schemaVersion: 1
technicalNotes: Current schema version: 1 (first versioned release)
Migration from unversioned: add schemaVersion=1, add missing dependencies:[] to stories that lack it
lib/migrate_prd.py must be idempotent (safe to run multiple times)

userStories.title: Add story tags field and --focus-tags CLI filter to spiral.sh
userStories.description: SPIRAL_FOCUS provides a theme hint but does not filter by structured metadata. A tags array on each story (e.g., ['frontend', 'auth', 'bugfix']) combined with a --focus-tags CLI flag lets users run targeted spiral sessions that only implement matching stories, supporting sprint planning and staged rollouts.
acceptanceCriteria: prd_schema.py accepts an optional tags field: a list of non-empty lowercase strings matching /^[a-z0-9_-]+$/
spiral.sh accepts --focus-tags 'frontend,auth' to restrict Phase I to stories whose tags contain at least one match
Stories without matching tags are skipped for the iteration (not failed, not retry-counted)
When --focus-tags is not set, all stories are eligible (no behavior change)
merge_stories.py auto-assigns tag 'bugfix' to stories where isTestFix=true
technicalNotes: jq filter: .userStories[] | select(.passes == false) | select((.tags // []) | any(. == "frontend"))
Tags are additive: a story with tags ['frontend', 'auth'] matches --focus-tags 'auth'
Research output schema should include optional tags field

userStories.title: Add HTML dashboard auto-refresh meta tag with configurable interval
userStories.description: The HTML metrics dashboard is static and requires manual browser reload. Adding a <meta http-equiv='refresh'> tag with interval from SPIRAL_DASHBOARD_REFRESH_SECS (default: 30) turns it into a live dashboard during active runs without requiring a web server.
acceptanceCriteria: SPIRAL_DASHBOARD_REFRESH_SECS is documented in spiral.config.sh template (default: 30)
Generated dashboard HTML includes <meta http-equiv='refresh' content='30'> (or configured value)
Setting SPIRAL_DASHBOARD_REFRESH_SECS=0 omits the meta tag entirely (static mode)

technicalNotes: Inject the meta tag in spiral_dashboard.py HTML generation function
<meta http-equiv='refresh' content='{interval}'> goes in <head>
Consider showing a JS countdown timer as a UX enhancement (optional)

userStories.title: Add SPIRAL_SKIP_STORY_IDS for permanent manual story exclusion
userStories.description: The _skipped flag handles auto-skip after MAX_RETRIES, but users sometimes want to permanently park a story (blocked externally, or descoped mid-run) without deleting it. SPIRAL_SKIP_STORY_IDS provides a comma-separated list of IDs always passed over without penalty.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral.config.sh template documents SPIRAL_SKIP_STORY_IDS with a commented example

jq filter: select(.id as $id | ($SKIP_IDS | split(",") | any(. == $id)) | not)
Manually-skipped stories appear in the HTML review report tagged as 'Skipped by user'

userStories.title: Merge parallel worker results.tsv files into main results.tsv
userStories.description: In parallel mode, each worker writes telemetry rows to its own worktree's results.tsv. After merge_worker_results.py finishes, the worker results.tsv files are abandoned ï¿½ only the main prd.json is updated. This means the main project's results.tsv is missing all telemetry from parallel runs, breaking spiral_dashboard.py accuracy and spiral_report.py analysis.
acceptanceCriteria: run_parallel_ralph.sh reads each worker's results.tsv after all workers complete
Rows from all worker results.tsv files are appended to the main project results.tsv
Header row is written only if main results.tsv does not already exist
Duplicate rows (same story_id + timestamp combination) are not inserted twice
spiral_dashboard.py shows correct total attempt count when re-run after a parallel session
technicalNotes: Worker worktrees live at $WORKTREE_BASE/worker-N/ ï¿½ each has its own results.tsv at that worktree root
run_parallel_ralph.sh already has a merge step for prd.json; add results.tsv merge immediately after
Use awk/tail to skip the header of each worker file before appending to main
Sort merged rows by timestamp column to maintain chronological order

userStories.title: Add disk space preflight check before creating N git worktrees
userStories.description: run_parallel_ralph.sh creates N git worktrees, each a full copy of the repository working tree. On large repos or machines with limited disk, this can silently fail mid-execution when disk fills up, leaving the project in an inconsistent state. A preflight check that estimates required space and aborts early with a clear message prevents this.
acceptanceCriteria: Before creating any worktree, compute estimated required bytes: repo_size_kb * RALPH_WORKERS
Check available disk space on the filesystem containing WORKTREE_BASE using df
If estimated_required > available_space * 0.9, abort with a clear error listing repo size, worker count, and free space
On Linux/macOS/Git-Bash the check uses POSIX df syntax; on systems where df fails it degrades gracefully
Check is skipped if SPIRAL_SKIP_DISK_CHECK=1 is set in environment
technicalNotes: Use: du -sk "$REPO_ROOT" to get repo size in KB
Use: df -k "$WORKTREE_BASE" | awk 'NR==2 {print $4}' for available KB
Git worktrees share .git objects ï¿½ actual disk use is ~working tree size, not full clone
Add the check near line 200 of run_parallel_ralph.sh before the worker creation loop

userStories.title: Add unit tests for merge_worker_results.py (parallel result promotion)
userStories.description: merge_worker_results.py is a critical path module that promotes passes=true, _skipped, _decomposed flags, and new sub-stories from N worker prd.json files back into the main prd.json. It has no test file. Bugs here silently lose work completed by parallel workers.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/test_merge_worker_results.py created with at least 8 test cases
Test: single worker with one passed story promotes it to main prd
Test: worker with _skipped story propagates skipped flag to main prd
Test: worker with _decomposed parent and new sub-stories ï¿½ both promoted correctly
Test: two workers both passing the same story ï¿½ no duplicate in main
Test: worker prd.json fails schema validation ï¿½ returns exit code 1, main prd unchanged
Test: worker file missing ï¿½ warning printed, other workers still processed
All new tests pass: uv run pytest tests/test_merge_worker_results.py -v
technicalNotes: Use tmp_path pytest fixture to create isolated prd.json files per test
Import merge_worker_results.main() directly or invoke via subprocess
Follow pattern of existing tests/test_merge.py for fixture setup style

userStories.title: Validate spiral.sh integer CLI arguments before arithmetic
userStories.description: spiral.sh performs bash integer arithmetic on --ralph-workers, --ralph-iters, --capacity-limit, and the positional max_iters argument. If a user passes a non-integer value, bash arithmetic fails with a cryptic 'arithmetic syntax error' deep in the loop. Validating integer flags immediately after parsing prevents this with a clear usage error.
acceptanceCriteria: After argument parsing, check MAX_SPIRAL_ITERS, RALPH_MAX_ITERS, RALPH_WORKERS, CAPACITY_LIMIT are valid integers
On invalid value, print: 'Error: --flag requires a positive integer, got: VALUE' and exit 1
TIME_LIMIT_MINS is also validated when --time-limit is used
Valid values pass through unchanged with no regression on normal usage
uv run pytest passes (all existing tests still green)
technicalNotes: Add validation block immediately after the while [[ $# -gt 0 ]] argument parsing loop in spiral.sh
Use bash regex: [[ "$RALPH_WORKERS" =~ ^[1-9][0-9]*$ ]] for positive integers
RALPH_WORKERS must be >= 1; MAX_SPIRAL_ITERS >= 1; CAPACITY_LIMIT >= 0

userStories.title: Add SPIRAL_RESEARCH_RETRIES to retry Phase R on transient Claude crash
userStories.description: Phase R spawns a Claude agent that writes _research_output.json. If the Claude process crashes (OOM kill, network timeout, API error) before writing the file, spiral.sh continues to Phase T with a stale or missing research output. Adding SPIRAL_RESEARCH_RETRIES (default 2) retries the Claude invocation before falling back.
acceptanceCriteria: SPIRAL_RESEARCH_RETRIES config variable defaults to 2 (read from spiral.config.sh or env)
After Phase R Claude invocation, check if _research_output.json was written and is valid JSON
If missing or invalid, retry the Claude invocation up to SPIRAL_RESEARCH_RETRIES times
Each retry prints: '[R] Research output missing or invalid ï¿½ retrying (attempt N/TOTAL)'
After all retries exhausted, print a warning and continue with previous iteration output or empty
Research succeeding on retry causes Phase T and M to proceed normally
technicalNotes: Check _research_output.json validity after claude invocation: python3 -c "import json; json.load(open('$RESEARCH_OUTPUT'))"
Add a retry loop in spiral.sh Phase R section wrapping the claude invocation command
Exponential backoff is not needed here ï¿½ retries are cheap since Claude starts fresh

userStories.title: Add unit tests for spiral_dashboard.py compute functions
userStories.description: spiral_dashboard.py contains compute_overview, compute_velocity, compute_model_performance, compute_status_breakdown, and compute_bottlenecks as pure data transformation functions with no test coverage. Bugs in these functions produce silently incorrect dashboard metrics that operators rely on for decision making.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/test_spiral_dashboard.py created with at least 10 test cases
Test compute_overview: empty inputs produce zero counts with no crash
Test compute_overview: partial inputs produce correct passed/pending/skipped counts
Test compute_velocity: rows from multiple iterations produce correct velocity per iteration
Test compute_model_performance: mixed model rows produce correct per-model success rates
Test elapsed time: valid ISO timestamps produce human-readable duration string

All tests pass: uv run pytest tests/test_spiral_dashboard.py -v
technicalNotes: Import compute_* functions directly from lib.spiral_dashboard (add sys.path insert)
Build minimal prd/results/retries fixtures as Python dicts ï¿½ no temp files needed

userStories.title: Add --prd flag to spiral.sh to specify alternate PRD file path
userStories.description: spiral.sh always uses prd.json in the current working directory. Users running spiral on monorepos with multiple prd files or non-standard locations must cd to specific directories or use symlinks. Adding a --prd flag matching ralph.sh's existing --prd flag would eliminate this friction and enable scripted multi-project automation.
acceptanceCriteria: --prd PATH flag accepted in spiral.sh argument parsing block
When --prd is given, PRD_FILE is set to the provided path instead of prd.json

bash spiral.sh --prd /other/project/prd.json --gate skip runs end-to-end correctly

userStories.title: Sort prd.json stories by priority and dependency level after Phase M merge
userStories.description: After Phase M (merge_stories.py) inserts new research stories, they appear in insertion order, not priority order. Since ralph picks the first story where passes=false, low-priority new stories inserted ahead of high-priority pending stories get implemented first. Re-sorting after every merge ensures ralph always works highest-priority first.
acceptanceCriteria: merge_stories.py sorts all userStories after merging: critical < high < medium < low
Within each priority tier, stories with fewer unmet dependencies come first
Stories with passes=true, _decomposed=true, or _skipped=true sort to the end
Sort is stable: stories with equal priority and dep count preserve relative insertion order
Existing unit tests for merge_stories.py still pass; one new test verifies sort order
technicalNotes: Add sort step at end of merge_stories.py main() before the atomic prd.json write
Priority rank: critical=0, high=1, medium=2, low=3
Dependency count tiebreaker: len(story.get('dependencies', []))
Python stable sort: stories.sort(key=lambda s: (done_rank(s), priority_rank(s), dep_count(s)))

userStories.title: Add SPIRAL_WORKER_MEMORY_LIMIT for per-worker V8 heap cap
userStories.description: All processes (orchestrator, research, workers) share the same SPIRAL_MEMORY_LIMIT for V8 --max-old-space-size. The orchestrator runs lightly while workers run full Claude CLI sessions. Separate limits let operators give workers more memory without over-allocating for the orchestrator, reducing OOM risk on memory-constrained machines.
acceptanceCriteria: SPIRAL_WORKER_MEMORY_LIMIT config variable added, defaulting to ${SPIRAL_MEMORY_LIMIT:-1024}
run_parallel_ralph.sh exports SPIRAL_MEMORY_LIMIT=$SPIRAL_WORKER_MEMORY_LIMIT before each worker launch
spiral.sh orchestrator continues using its own SPIRAL_MEMORY_LIMIT for research phase NODE_OPTIONS
Worker ralph.sh processes inherit the overridden SPIRAL_MEMORY_LIMIT via environment
templates/spiral.config.example.sh documents SPIRAL_WORKER_MEMORY_LIMIT with example value 2048
technicalNotes: In run_parallel_ralph.sh, before spawning each worker: export SPIRAL_MEMORY_LIMIT="${SPIRAL_WORKER_MEMORY_LIMIT:-$SPIRAL_MEMORY_LIMIT}"
ralph.sh already sets NODE_OPTIONS from SPIRAL_MEMORY_LIMIT so only the env var needs to change
Add SPIRAL_WORKER_MEMORY_LIMIT=2048 with comment to spiral.config.example.sh

userStories.title: Log Gemini Phase R failure reason (rate-limit vs error vs empty output)
userStories.description: Phase R uses || true to swallow all Gemini CLI errors. When Gemini returns empty output, spiral.sh logs 'Gemini web research returned empty' but gives no reason ï¿½ making it impossible to distinguish a 429 rate limit, API key error, network failure, or legitimate empty output. Capturing stderr enables targeted recovery messages.
acceptanceCriteria: Gemini invocation captures stderr to a temp file: gemini ... 2>"$GEMINI_ERR_TMP" || true
If GEMINI_RESEARCH is empty and stderr contains '429' or 'rate limit', print: '[R] Gemini rate-limited ï¿½ Claude will browse URLs directly'
If stderr contains 'PERMISSION_DENIED' or 'API key', print: '[R] Gemini auth error ï¿½ check GEMINI_API_KEY'
If stderr is non-empty but unknown pattern, print the first line as diagnostic context
If GEMINI_RESEARCH is non-empty, no stderr check is performed
Temp file is cleaned up after the check in all code paths
technicalNotes: Replace: GEMINI_RESEARCH=$(gemini ... 2>/dev/null || true) with stderr capture pattern
GEMINI_ERR_TMP=$(mktemp); then gemini ... 2>"$GEMINI_ERR_TMP" || true; then check file
Rate limit detection pattern: grep -qi '429\|RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED\|rate.limit\|quota' "$GEMINI_ERR_TMP"

userStories.title: Isolate parallel worker failures so one crash does not abort sibling workers
userStories.description: run_parallel_ralph.sh spawns N workers in background subshells. If a worker exits non-zero, bash set -e behavior in the outer script can propagate the error and abort remaining workers. Workers that fail due to git conflicts, OOM, or network errors should be reported individually while sibling workers continue to completion.
acceptanceCriteria: Each background worker PID is collected into an array after launch
After all workers are spawned, iterate per-PID: wait $PID; worker_status=$?
If a worker exits non-zero, print: '[parallel] Worker N exited with status S ï¿½ continuing remaining workers'
Failed worker worktree is cleaned up on the same path as successful workers
merge_worker_results.py is still called with all worker prd paths ï¿½ missing workers emit warnings but do not abort the merge
If all workers fail, exit run_parallel_ralph.sh with a non-zero exit code
technicalNotes: Replace any bulk 'wait' call with a per-PID wait loop: for pid in "${WORKER_PIDS[@]}"; do wait "$pid" || failed=$((failed+1)); done
Track failed_count; if failed_count == RALPH_WORKERS, exit 1 after cleanup
The existing EXIT/INT/TERM trap handles cleanup ï¿½ this change only affects normal worker exit code handling

userStories.title: Dashboard: add per-story attempt history drilldown via HTML details element
userStories.description: spiral_dashboard.py shows aggregate metrics but not which specific attempts were made for a given story. When debugging a repeatedly-failing story, engineers must manually parse results.tsv. Adding an expandable drilldown per story (all attempt rows with model, status, duration, commit) makes failure analysis self-contained in the dashboard.
acceptanceCriteria: HTML dashboard has a Stories section with one row per story showing id, title, status indicator
Each story row has a Details toggle (HTML <details> element) expanding to show attempt rows from results.tsv for that story_id
Attempt rows show: timestamp, model, tool, status, duration_sec, retry_num, commit_sha
Stories with no attempt history show 'No attempts recorded' in the expanded section
Feature uses only HTML/CSS with no JavaScript dependency
Dashboard still generates correctly when results.tsv is missing (Stories section shows prd data only)
technicalNotes: Group results rows by story_id in a new compute_story_attempts() function before HTML generation
Use HTML5 <details><summary> accordion elements ï¿½ zero JavaScript required
Insert Stories drilldown section after the overview cards and before the velocity chart

userStories.title: Store _failureReason field in prd.json when story is skipped or decomposed
userStories.description: When a story exceeds MAX_RETRIES and is skipped, the reason (time budget exceeded, quality gate, all retries exhausted) is written only to progress.txt as free text. After many iterations, identifying why a specific story was skipped requires grepping progress.txt. Storing a structured _failureReason in prd.json makes this queryable and visible in the dashboard.
acceptanceCriteria: When ralph.sh skips a story after MAX_RETRIES, set _failureReason: 'MAX_RETRIES exhausted (quality gate failed)' or similar
When a story is skipped due to time budget exceeded, set _failureReason: 'TIME_BUDGET_EXCEEDED (Xs > Ys limit)'
_failureReason is preserved when merge_worker_results.py promotes skipped stories from workers to main prd
spiral_dashboard.py displays _failureReason in a tooltip or the story drilldown section
prd_schema.py validates _failureReason as an optional string field (no required update to existing stories)
technicalNotes: In ralph.sh, when writing _skipped=true via jq, pipe a second jq to set ._failureReason
Escape reason string for JSON: use jq --arg reason "$REASON" '.field = $arg'
Add optional _failureReason: { type: 'string' } to prd_schema.py STORY_SCHEMA

userStories.title: Wizard: dry-run SPIRAL_VALIDATE_CMD before writing to spiral.config.sh
userStories.description: The /spiral-init wizard asks for SPIRAL_VALIDATE_CMD but never verifies the command works before writing it to config. If the user enters a wrong path or missing executable, every Phase V validation will fail and spiral will loop forever treating all tests as broken. A quick dry-run during setup catches this at the source.
acceptanceCriteria: After user enters SPIRAL_VALIDATE_CMD, wizard runs: timeout 30 bash -c "$SPIRAL_VALIDATE_CMD" and checks exit code
If exit 0: print '[wizard] Validate command verified ï¿½ writing to config'
If non-zero exit or timeout: print 'Command returned exit N ï¿½ use anyway? (y/N)' and wait for input
If exit 127 (command not found): print specific error '[wizard] Command not found: <cmd>' with install hint
User pressing Enter (default N) re-prompts for the command; y accepts as-is
Empty SPIRAL_VALIDATE_CMD input skips the dry-run and uses the detected project default
technicalNotes: Add dry-run check in ralph/skills/spiral-init.md after the SPIRAL_VALIDATE_CMD input block
Use timeout to prevent hanging on infinite-loop test suites
Exit code 127 is the standard POSIX shell 'command not found' code

userStories.title: Add integration test for run_parallel_ralph.sh with mock worker scripts
userStories.description: run_parallel_ralph.sh (622 lines) is the most complex orchestration script and has zero automated test coverage. It creates git worktrees, partitions prd.json, spawns background processes, merges results, and cleans up ï¿½ many steps that can fail independently. A minimal integration test using mock worker scripts validates the orchestration without real Claude agents.
acceptanceCriteria: tests/test_parallel_orchestration.py or tests/test_parallel_integration.sh created
Test setup: creates a temp git repo with a minimal prd.json (3 stories) configured for 2 workers
Mock SPIRAL_RALPH: a bash script that marks one story passes=true in its worker prd.json and exits 0
After run_parallel_ralph.sh, main prd.json has the story marked passes=true
After completion, worktrees are cleaned up from the filesystem
Second scenario: one mock worker exits 1, the other succeeds ï¿½ successful worker's result is still merged
Test is runnable via: uv run pytest tests/test_parallel_orchestration.py -v
technicalNotes: Use pytest with subprocess.run to call run_parallel_ralph.sh with a custom SPIRAL_RALPH env pointing to a mock script
Mock ralph.sh script: reads its worker prd.json, finds first pending story, sets passes=true via jq, exits 0
Requires git to be available in the test environment (already a SPIRAL prerequisite)

userStories.title: Add --version flag to spiral.sh showing installed git version
userStories.description: There is no way to query which version of SPIRAL is installed. Users updating their installation or reporting bugs cannot identify the exact version. Adding --version that prints the git describe output from SPIRAL_HOME makes version identification trivial and enables bug reports to include version context.
acceptanceCriteria: --version flag added to spiral.sh argument parsing
When --version is given, print 'SPIRAL version <git-describe or commit SHA>' and exit 0
Version derived from: git -C "$SPIRAL_HOME" describe --tags --always 2>/dev/null
If SPIRAL_HOME is not a git repo (zip install), print: 'SPIRAL version unknown (not a git repository)'
--version is documented in --help output
technicalNotes: Add '--version) echo "SPIRAL version $(git -C "$SPIRAL_HOME" describe --tags --always 2>/dev/null || echo unknown)"; exit 0 ;;' early in argument parsing
Use --dirty=+ suffix to indicate uncommitted changes: git describe --tags --always --dirty=+
Expose as SPIRAL_VERSION env var for checkpoint.json recording

userStories.title: Auto-detect SPIRAL_STORY_PREFIX from existing prd.json IDs at wizard init
userStories.description: When /spiral-init runs on a project that already has a prd.json with custom story IDs (e.g., FEAT-001, TICKET-042), the wizard defaults to SPIRAL_STORY_PREFIX=US, causing new stories to get inconsistent IDs. Auto-detecting the prefix from existing IDs and pre-filling the wizard improves consistency without user effort.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral-init wizard scans existing prd.json userStories[*].id before prompting for STORY_PREFIX
If existing IDs share a consistent prefix pattern ([A-Z]+-NNN), extract that prefix
Pre-fill the SPIRAL_STORY_PREFIX prompt with the detected prefix
If no existing stories or no consistent prefix found, default to 'US' as before
User can still override the pre-filled value
Detected prefix is written to spiral.config.sh as SPIRAL_STORY_PREFIX=<value>
technicalNotes: Detection step in spiral-init.md: DETECTED_PREFIX=$(cat prd.json | jq -r '.userStories[0].id // "" | split("-")[0]' 2>/dev/null)
Validate detected prefix matches ^[A-Z]+$ before using it
Only use detected prefix if at least 2 existing stories share the same prefix

userStories.title: Emit SPIRAL_VERSION to _checkpoint.json for version mismatch detection on resume
userStories.description: When a SPIRAL session is interrupted and resumed later, the installed SPIRAL version may have changed (e.g., user git-pulled an update). If the new version changed checkpoint format or phase behavior, resuming from an old checkpoint can cause subtle bugs. Recording the version in _checkpoint.json enables detection and a warning on mismatch.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral.sh derives SPIRAL_VERSION at startup: git -C "$SPIRAL_HOME" describe --tags --always 2>/dev/null || echo 'unknown'
When writing _checkpoint.json, include a spiralVersion field with the current SPIRAL_VERSION
When loading an existing checkpoint, compare its spiralVersion field with current SPIRAL_VERSION
If versions differ, print: '[checkpoint] WARNING: checkpoint written by SPIRAL <old>, current is <new>'
Warning is informational only and does not abort execution
uv run pytest passes with checkpoint tests updated to include the spiralVersion field
technicalNotes: Add SPIRAL_VERSION derivation near the top of spiral.sh after SPIRAL_HOME is set
The checkpoint read/write is in spiral.sh inline or lib/state_machine.py ï¿½ update whichever writes the checkpoint dict
Version comparison uses string equality; no semver parsing needed

userStories.title: Add SIGCHLD trap to spiral.sh to reap zombie worker processes
userStories.description: When parallel workers exit, their process table entries persist as zombies until the parent calls wait(). Without a SIGCHLD handler, SPIRAL accumulates zombie workers during long parallel runs, eventually exhausting PID space. A `trap 'wait -n 2>/dev/null; true' SIGCHLD` in spiral.sh immediately reaps terminated children on signal delivery.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral.sh installs a SIGCHLD trap that calls `wait -n 2>/dev/null; true` to reap all available zombie children
After N parallel workers complete, `ps aux | grep defunct` shows zero zombie entries from spiral.sh children
The trap does not interfere with the existing SIGINT/SIGTERM shutdown trap (US-017)
technicalNotes: Use `trap 'while wait -n 2>/dev/null; do :; done; true' SIGCHLD` to drain all zombies in one handler invocation
`wait -n` (bash 4.3+) waits for any one child; the `true` suppresses non-zero exit when no children remain
Source: http://www.microhowto.info/howto/reap_zombie_processes_using_a_sigchld_handler.html

userStories.title: Lock git worktrees during active worker execution to prevent prune races
userStories.description: `git worktree prune` can silently remove a worktree whose directory was briefly absent during worker setup, causing a live worker to operate on a deleted worktree. The `git worktree lock` command prevents automatic pruning of an in-use worktree. SPIRAL should lock each worktree at worker launch and unlock it on worker exit.
acceptanceCriteria: run_parallel_ralph.sh calls `git worktree lock .spiral-workers/worker-N --reason 'worker-N running story $STORY_ID'` immediately after `git worktree add`
`git worktree unlock .spiral-workers/worker-N` is called in the worker's EXIT trap so it fires even on crash
Running `git worktree prune` while a worker is active does NOT remove the locked worktree
technicalNotes: `git worktree lock [--reason <string>] <path>` — officially supported since Git 2.7
Place unlock in the EXIT trap alongside the existing SIGINT/SIGTERM handlers
Source: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-worktree#Documentation/git-worktree.txt-lock

userStories.title: Guard against 'branch already checked out' before git worktree add
userStories.description: Git refuses to create a worktree for a branch already checked out in another worktree, causing a hard failure that crashes the worker launch loop. Before calling `git worktree add`, SPIRAL must verify the target branch is free or use detached HEAD mode to avoid the collision entirely.
acceptanceCriteria: Before `git worktree add`, run_parallel_ralph.sh calls `git worktree list --porcelain` and checks if the target branch appears in any existing worktree
If the branch is in use, the script falls back to `git worktree add --detach .spiral-workers/worker-N <commit>` (detached HEAD mode)
A clear log message explains the detached HEAD fallback when it occurs
technicalNotes: `git worktree list --porcelain` outputs `branch refs/heads/<name>` per worktree; grep for the target branch name
Detached HEAD worktrees (`git worktree add --detach`) are not subject to the branch-in-use check
Source: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-worktree#_notes (branch already checked out limitation)

userStories.title: Handle HTTP 529 overloaded_error with separate backoff strategy from 429
userStories.description: The Anthropic API returns 529 (overloaded_error) when its servers are under load — distinct from 429 (rate_limit_error). The existing 429 handler uses the `retry-after` header, which is absent on 529 responses. A separate handler with exponential backoff and jitter (2s base, 30s cap, 10% jitter) should be applied for 529 errors to avoid immediate re-hammering.
acceptanceCriteria: ralph.sh detects HTTP 529 or `error.type == overloaded_error` in Claude CLI output separately from 429 detection
On 529, ralph.sh applies exponential backoff: `sleep $((base * 2**attempt + RANDOM % jitter_range))` up to 30s cap
529 events are logged to `.spiral/spiral_events.jsonl` as `api_overloaded` with retry attempt number
529 retries do NOT consume a story's `_retryCount` (unlike implementation failures)
technicalNotes: Anthropic API returns 529 for system-wide overload; distinct from per-account 429 with retry-after header
Recommended backoff: base=2s, cap=30s, jitter=10% of delay: `jitter=$(( (delay * 10) / 100 ))`
Source: https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/errors and https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/rate-limits

userStories.title: Prune stale git worktree admin records on worker completion
userStories.description: When a worktree directory is removed without `git worktree remove` (e.g., after a crash), Git retains orphaned admin records in `.git/worktrees/`. These accumulate over many SPIRAL runs. Adding `git worktree prune` to the post-worker cleanup step removes dangling references and keeps `.git/worktrees/` clean.
acceptanceCriteria: After each worker completes, spiral.sh (or run_parallel_ralph.sh cleanup hook) calls `git worktree prune`
`git worktree list` shows no stale entries after N completed parallel runs
If `git worktree prune` is run while another worker is locked, it does not remove the locked worktree
technicalNotes: `git worktree prune --dry-run` logs what would be removed; useful for debugging without side effects
`git worktree prune --expire now` removes entries with no grace period for CI cleanliness
Source: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-worktree#Documentation/git-worktree.txt-prune

userStories.title: Detect streaming Claude API overload errors returned with HTTP 200 status
userStories.description: When Claude CLI streams a response, an overloaded_error can arrive in the first chunk while the HTTP status is 200 OK. Standard exit-code-based retry logic silently misses these errors, causing story implementation to proceed with empty output. SPIRAL needs to inspect streamed output for `"type": "error"` in the first response chunk.
acceptanceCriteria: ralph.sh (or its Claude invocation wrapper) inspects Claude CLI stdout for the pattern `"type":"error"` in the first line/chunk
When detected, the overloaded_error is treated identically to an HTTP 529 (apply 529 backoff, log `api_overloaded` event)
A bats test verifies the detection with a mocked claude binary that emits a canned overload JSON body
technicalNotes: Claude CLI streams NDJSON; first line may be `{"type":"error","error":{"type":"overloaded_error","message":"..."}}`
Capture first line: `first_line=$(claude ... | { IFS= read -r line; echo "$line"; cat > /dev/null; echo "$line"; })`
Source: https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/errors (streaming errors section)

userStories.title: Apply jitter to all API retries in ralph.sh to prevent thundering herd
userStories.description: When multiple parallel SPIRAL workers hit a 429/529 error simultaneously and retry after the same fixed delay, they re-hit the API limit together (thundering herd). Encapsulating retry logic with per-worker random jitter in a shared `lib/spiral_retry.sh` bash function prevents synchronized retries across workers.
acceptanceCriteria: A new `lib/spiral_retry.sh` exports a `spiral_api_retry <cmd>` function that wraps any Claude CLI invocation with retry+jitter logic
Each retry delay adds `RANDOM % SPIRAL_RETRY_JITTER_S` seconds (configurable, default 5) to the base backoff
spiral_retry.sh is sourced by both spiral.sh Phase R and ralph.sh Phase I
SPIRAL_RETRY_JITTER_S is documented in templates/spiral.config.example.sh
technicalNotes: Jitter formula: `sleep_s=$(( base_delay + (RANDOM % jitter_range) ))` where RANDOM is bash built-in (0-32767)
Each worker's RANDOM seed differs by PID: `RANDOM=$$` at script start produces worker-unique sequences
Source: https://docs.anthropic.com/en/api/rate-limits (retry recommendations)

userStories.title: Add SPIRAL_RUN_ID correlation field to all structured log entries
userStories.description: When multiple SPIRAL runs share a `spiral_events.jsonl` file or a metrics file, there is no way to filter entries from a single run. Generating a `SPIRAL_RUN_ID` UUID at startup and including it in every structured output row enables complete run-level filtering without modifying file paths.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral.sh generates `SPIRAL_RUN_ID=$(uuidgen 2>/dev/null || printf '%x%x' $(date +%s) $RANDOM)` at startup and exports it
Every row written to `spiral_events.jsonl` includes `"run_id": "$SPIRAL_RUN_ID"`
Every row written to `results.tsv` includes `run_id` as a new column
SPIRAL_RUN_ID is written to `_checkpoint.json` so it persists across crash-resume cycles
technicalNotes: Equivalent to OTel's `Resource.service.instance.id` — enables filtering all events from one run
Write to checkpoint: `jq '. + {run_id: env.SPIRAL_RUN_ID}' _checkpoint.json > _checkpoint.json.tmp && mv _checkpoint.json.tmp _checkpoint.json`
Source: https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/otel/logs/data-model/ (log correlation IDs)

userStories.title: Add bats test scaffold for spiral.sh phase transitions
userStories.description: spiral.sh has no shell-level tests — only Python unit tests for the lib/ utilities. A bats-core test suite validates that each phase flag (--gate skip, --gate proceed, --skip-research) produces the expected exit codes and output files without requiring a live Claude API, providing test coverage for the orchestration layer.
acceptanceCriteria: test/spiral_phases.bats exists with bats-core, bats-assert, and bats-mock as git submodules in test/
`setup_file()` creates a temp REPO_ROOT with a minimal prd.json and a mocked `claude` binary on PATH
Tests cover: `--gate skip` exits 0, `--gate quit` exits 0 with no implementation, `--skip-research` skips Phase R
`bats test/spiral_phases.bats` runs in CI without any real API key or network access
technicalNotes: bats-core setup: `git submodule add https://github.com/bats-core/bats-core test/bats`
Mock claude binary: create a bash stub via bats-mock that writes canned _research_output.json and exits 0
Use `run bash spiral.sh 1 --gate skip` and `assert_success`; check artifacts with `assert_file_exists`
Source: https://bats-core.readthedocs.io/en/stable/writing-tests.html

userStories.title: Add bats tests for lib/ shell utility scripts
userStories.description: validate_preflight.sh, memory-pressure-check.sh, and spiral_assert.sh have no automated tests. bats-core provides `run`, `assert_success`, `assert_failure`, and `assert_output` helpers ideal for testing these small shell scripts in isolation with mocked inputs, catching regressions in preflight checks.
acceptanceCriteria: test/lib/validate_preflight.bats tests that validate_preflight.sh exits non-zero when a required tool (jq, git) is absent from PATH
test/lib/memory_pressure.bats tests that memory-pressure-check.sh exits 1 when free memory (mocked via /proc/meminfo stub) is below threshold
test/lib/spiral_assert.bats tests assert_file_exists and assert_json_key helpers with known fixtures
All lib bats tests pass with `bats test/lib/`
technicalNotes: Mock missing binaries: `mock_create; mock_set_status $mock 1; export PATH="$(dirname $mock):$PATH"`
bats-file library provides `assert_file_exists`, `assert_file_not_empty` assertions out of the box
Source: https://bats-core.readthedocs.io/en/stable/usage.html

userStories.title: Add workflow_dispatch inputs to SPIRAL GitHub Actions workflow for manual reruns
userStories.description: The existing GitHub Actions CI workflow runs SPIRAL only on push/PR events. Adding `workflow_dispatch` with inputs (`max_iterations`, `gate`, `dry_run`) lets operators manually trigger a SPIRAL run from the GitHub UI — useful for a research-only pass or re-running after a transient API failure without pushing a commit.
acceptanceCriteria: `.github/workflows/spiral.yml` adds a `workflow_dispatch:` trigger with inputs: `max_iterations` (string, default '5'), `gate` (choice: proceed/skip/quit, default proceed), `dry_run` (boolean, default false)
The workflow step passes inputs to spiral.sh: `bash spiral.sh ${{ inputs.max_iterations }} --gate ${{ inputs.gate }}`
When `dry_run` is true, the workflow overrides gate to `skip` regardless of the gate input
The manual trigger appears in the GitHub Actions UI under 'Run workflow'
technicalNotes: workflow_dispatch supports up to 25 inputs with types: string, choice, boolean, environment
Inputs accessed via `${{ inputs.max_iterations }}` or `${{ github.event.inputs.max_iterations }}`
Source: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/using-workflows/events-that-trigger-workflows#workflow_dispatch

userStories.title: Dashboard: detect and report orphaned git worktrees vs live worker PIDs
userStories.description: After a crash or hard kill, `.spiral-workers/worker-N` directories may remain as orphaned worktrees whose worker processes are dead. spiral_dashboard.py should cross-reference `git worktree list` against live worker PID files (via `os.kill(pid, 0)`) and surface any orphaned worktrees with a suggested prune command.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral_dashboard.py reads active worker PID files from `.spiral-workers/worker-N/worker.pid`
For each PID file, it checks liveness with `os.kill(pid, 0)` — `ProcessLookupError` means dead PID
For each dead-PID worktree, the dashboard displays an 'ORPHANED' status with the worktree path and `git worktree remove <path>` suggested command
The orphan check runs at dashboard startup (not on a background timer)
technicalNotes: `subprocess.run(['git', 'worktree', 'list', '--porcelain'], capture_output=True)` gives structured worktree info
`os.kill(pid, 0)` raises `ProcessLookupError` if PID is dead, `PermissionError` if alive but not owned by us
Source: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-worktree

userStories.title: disown background workers in spiral.sh to decouple from terminal SIGHUP
userStories.description: Background workers launched with `&` in spiral.sh receive SIGHUP when the controlling terminal closes (e.g., SSH disconnect), killing all workers mid-story. Calling `disown $!` after each worker launch removes it from the shell job table, preventing HUP propagation. Worker lifecycle is then tracked exclusively through PID files.
acceptanceCriteria: In run_parallel_ralph.sh, `disown $worker_pid` is called immediately after launching each worker with `&` and capturing `$!`
Closing the SSH session that launched SPIRAL does NOT send SIGHUP to running worker processes
Worker PIDs are written to `.spiral-workers/worker-N/worker.pid` before disown so they remain trackable
The existing SIGINT trap in spiral.sh still terminates workers explicitly when Ctrl+C is pressed (via `kill $worker_pids`)
technicalNotes: `disown $!` removes the most recently backgrounded job from the shell's job table without killing it
Pattern: `start_worker & worker_pid=$!; echo $worker_pid > worker.pid; disown $worker_pid`
Source: https://mywiki.wooledge.org/ProcessManagement

userStories.title: Emit JUnit XML from bats runs for GitHub Actions test summary
userStories.description: bats-core natively supports `--formatter junit` output, which GitHub Actions renders as a test summary panel with pass/fail counts per test case. Adding a JUnit XML output step to the CI workflow provides per-test visibility in the PR checks panel alongside the existing pytest JUnit output.
acceptanceCriteria: The GitHub Actions CI workflow runs `bats --formatter tap --report-formatter junit -o test-reports/ test/` in a dedicated step
The step uploads `test-reports/*.xml` as a workflow artifact
GitHub Actions test summary shows the bats results panel with individual test names and pass/fail status
`test-reports/` is listed in .gitignore
technicalNotes: bats invocation: `bats --formatter tap --report-formatter junit -o test-reports/ test/`
Use `dorny/test-reporter@v1` action to parse JUnit XML and post PR annotations
Source: https://bats-core.readthedocs.io/en/stable/usage.html#reporters

userStories.title: Upload SPIRAL output artifacts (prd.json, spiral_events.jsonl) in CI workflow
userStories.description: SPIRAL generates key output files (prd.json, _research_output.json, results.tsv, spiral_events.jsonl) during a CI run but they are lost when the runner terminates. Uploading them as workflow artifacts with `actions/upload-artifact@v4` preserves intermediate state for post-run debugging without checking generated files into git.
acceptanceCriteria: A post-run step in `.github/workflows/spiral.yml` uses `actions/upload-artifact@v4` to upload `prd.json`, `.spiral/_research_output.json`, `results.tsv`, and `.spiral/spiral_events.jsonl`
Artifact name includes the run ID: `spiral-outputs-${{ github.run_id }}`
The upload step runs with `if: always()` so artifacts are preserved even on failure
Artifacts are retained for 7 days (set via `retention-days: 7`)
technicalNotes: actions/upload-artifact@v4 supports multi-path globs via YAML block scalar under `path:`
As of Feb 2026, v4 supports `archive: false` for non-zipped uploads (faster for text files)
Source: https://github.com/actions/upload-artifact and https://docs.github.com/en/actions/tutorials/store-and-share-data

userStories.title: Add per-worker execution timeout using `timeout --kill-after` in run_parallel_ralph.sh
userStories.description: Workers that hang indefinitely (e.g., waiting on a stalled Claude CLI subprocess) currently block the entire parallel batch with no recovery path. Wrapping each worker invocation with `timeout --kill-after=60s ${SPIRAL_WORKER_TIMEOUT:-600}s` provides a two-phase SIGTERM→SIGKILL sequence and returns exit code 124 on expiry, allowing the coordinator to distinguish a timeout from a crash and requeue the story.
acceptanceCriteria: SPIRAL_WORKER_TIMEOUT env var (default 600s) controls per-worker wall-clock limit
Worker process tree receives SIGTERM first, then SIGKILL after 60s grace period
Exit code 124 from timeout is detected and logged as a distinct 'timeout' failure reason in results.tsv
Timed-out stories are reset to pending for the next iteration rather than counted as a retry
Integration test: a mock worker that sleeps 700s is killed within SPIRAL_WORKER_TIMEOUT+75s
technicalNotes: Use `timeout --kill-after=60s ${SPIRAL_WORKER_TIMEOUT:-600}s bash ralph.sh ...` in run_parallel_ralph.sh
Detect exit code 124 and write {"_failureReason": "worker_timeout"} to prd.json story entry
bash's built-in timeout may lack --kill-after on some MSYS2 builds; fall back to a background SIGKILL watchdog if GNU timeout is unavailable
Coordinate with SIGCHLD trap (pending story) to avoid double-reaping

userStories.title: Add SPIRAL_VALIDATE_TIMEOUT to bound Phase V test-suite execution time
userStories.description: Phase V runs the full test suite via SPIRAL_VALIDATE_CMD with no deadline. If a test hangs (e.g., an integration test waiting on an unavailable network resource), the entire SPIRAL loop stalls indefinitely. Adding SPIRAL_VALIDATE_TIMEOUT (default 300s) wraps the validate command with a timeout and surfaces a clear failure message rather than a silent hang.
acceptanceCriteria: SPIRAL_VALIDATE_TIMEOUT env var (default 300s) limits Phase V wall-clock time
When Phase V times out, spiral_events.jsonl receives a phase_timeout event with duration_ms
Phase V timeout is treated as a test failure (not a crash), allowing the loop to continue to Phase C
A warning message is printed: 'Phase V timed out after Xs — treating as validation failure'
SPIRAL_VALIDATE_TIMEOUT=0 disables the timeout (unlimited, matching current behaviour)
technicalNotes: Apply `timeout ${SPIRAL_VALIDATE_TIMEOUT:-300}s bash -c "$SPIRAL_VALIDATE_CMD"` in spiral.sh Phase V block
Exit code 124 maps to a synthetic test failure report so Phase C sees a failing state
Document in spiral.config.sh template with a note that slow integration test suites may need a higher value

userStories.title: Detect git merge conflicts with `git merge-tree` before parallel worker integration
userStories.description: When multiple workers implement stories that touch the same files, merging them back to main can produce conflicts that are only discovered after all workers have finished. Using `git merge-tree $(git merge-base HEAD main) HEAD main` as a non-destructive dry-run before each worker pushes allows early conflict detection and targeted requeuing of only the conflicting stories without discarding sibling workers' results.
acceptanceCriteria: After each worker completes Phase I, run `git merge-tree` dry-run against the main branch before git push
If merge-tree reports conflicts, the worker marks its stories as pending (not failed) and logs the conflicting file paths
The coordinator in run_parallel_ralph.sh surfaces per-worker conflict status in spiral_events.jsonl
Non-conflicting workers continue to merge normally; only conflicting workers are requeued
bats integration test covers the conflict-detected requeue path with a fixture repo
technicalNotes: Command: `git merge-tree $(git merge-base HEAD "$MAIN_BRANCH") HEAD "$MAIN_BRANCH"` — exit code 0 = no conflicts, non-zero = conflicts
`git merge-tree` is available in Git 2.38+ with improved output; fall back to a test merge + reset for older Git versions
See github.com/clash-sh/clash for a reference implementation pattern in a parallel-agent context
Conflicts reset story state to 'pending' with _failureReason: 'merge_conflict' to distinguish from implementation failures

userStories.title: Add `spiral.sh --replay STORY_ID` to re-run a single story without full loop
userStories.description: Developers frequently need to re-run a single story without triggering the full 7-phase loop, resetting the story's pass state, and waiting through Phases R/T/M/G. A `spiral.sh --replay US-XXX` mode sets only the target story to pending, skips Phases R/T/M/G, runs Phase I for that story only in an isolated worktree, then validates and reports. This reduces the inner feedback loop from minutes to seconds.
acceptanceCriteria: `spiral.sh --replay US-XXX` accepts a single story ID and runs only Phases I+V+C for that story
All other stories remain untouched in prd.json during replay
Replay creates an isolated git worktree `.spiral-replay-{STORY_ID}` so main branch is not polluted
Phase G (human gate) is automatically skipped in replay mode
Replay result (pass/fail + log path) is printed on exit and written to spiral_events.jsonl
Error with exit 1 if the story ID does not exist in prd.json
technicalNotes: Extract the single-story execution path already present in ralph.sh; replay mode sets STORY_IDS=US-XXX before Phase I
Replay worktree is cleaned up on successful pass; preserved on failure for inspection
`main.py` CLI (pending story) should expose `spiral replay` as a subcommand for symmetry

userStories.title: Add UNIX /proc/meminfo memory pressure adapter for cross-platform watchdog parity
userStories.description: The current memory pressure watchdog (memory-watchdog.ps1) is PowerShell-only, leaving UNIX/Linux/WSL environments without adaptive memory signals. Adding a Bash adapter that reads MemAvailable from /proc/meminfo (Linux) or uses vm_stat on macOS provides the same JSON signal file that spiral.sh already consumes, giving cross-platform parity with no changes to the main loop.
acceptanceCriteria: New script lib/memory-watchdog.sh detects platform (Linux vs macOS) and reads available memory
On Linux: reads MemAvailable from /proc/meminfo (not MemFree — MemAvailable accounts for reclaimable cache)
On macOS: reads Pages free from vm_stat, converts page size to MB
Outputs the same JSON signal format that memory-pressure-check.sh already expects
spiral.sh auto-selects the watchdog based on platform: .ps1 on Windows, .sh on UNIX
Unit test covers the platform-detection branch and mock /proc/meminfo parsing
technicalNotes: MemAvailable field (not MemFree) is the correct metric — Red Hat KB 406773 explains why MemFree underestimates available memory
Cross-platform pattern: `if [ -f /proc/meminfo ]; then awk '/MemAvailable/ {print int($2/1024)}' /proc/meminfo; else vm_stat | awk '/Pages free/ {print int($3*4096/1048576)}'; fi`
The JSON signal file path should be configurable (SPIRAL_MEMORY_SIGNAL_FILE) so both watchdogs write to the same location

userStories.title: Add SPIRAL_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK for per-phase-transition HTTP POST notifications
userStories.description: The pending SPIRAL_ON_COMPLETE hook fires only at run completion. Teams operating SPIRAL in long-running unattended sessions need real-time phase-transition notifications (e.g., 'Phase G awaiting approval', 'Phase V failed') delivered to Slack, Teams, or a custom endpoint. Adding SPIRAL_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK sends a JSON POST at each phase start/end, enabling live monitoring without polling the log file.
acceptanceCriteria: SPIRAL_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK env var accepts an HTTPS URL
A POST is sent at the start and end of each phase (R, T, M, G, I, V, C) with JSON body: {run_id, phase, event: start|end, status: ok|failed|skipped, timestamp_iso, iteration}
Webhook POST failures are non-fatal: log a warning and continue the loop
SPIRAL_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK_TIMEOUT (default 5s) bounds each POST so the loop is not blocked
Phase G approval-pending event body includes the gate report file path
curl is used for the POST to avoid additional Python dependencies
technicalNotes: Implement as a Bash function `notify_webhook()` called at each phase boundary in spiral.sh
JSON body constructed with jq for correct escaping of special characters
SPIRAL_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK_HEADERS optional env var allows Authorization: Bearer token header
Slack Incoming Webhooks accept this JSON format natively; Teams uses a similar shape

userStories.title: Add max diff size guard in ralph.sh to warn on oversized story implementations
userStories.description: AI-generated implementations that produce very large diffs (>500 lines changed) are frequently low quality — the model has gone off-scope, added unnecessary boilerplate, or implemented far more than the story required. A configurable SPIRAL_MAX_DIFF_LINES guard in ralph.sh detects oversized diffs before committing and marks the story for review or automatic retry with a tighter prompt.
acceptanceCriteria: SPIRAL_MAX_DIFF_LINES env var (default 500) sets the maximum allowed changed lines per story
After Phase I implementation, ralph.sh counts total changed lines via `git diff --stat HEAD`
If changed lines exceed SPIRAL_MAX_DIFF_LINES, the story is marked with _failureReason: 'oversized_diff' and the commit is not made
The oversized diff stat (files changed, insertions, deletions) is logged to spiral_events.jsonl
SPIRAL_MAX_DIFF_LINES=0 disables the guard (matches current behaviour)
Unit test verifies the line count extraction from `git diff --stat` output
technicalNotes: Use `git diff --stat | tail -1` to get total insertions+deletions summary line
Parse with awk: extract the numbers from '3 files changed, 450 insertions(+), 120 deletions(-)'
Consider a separate SPIRAL_MAX_DIFF_LINES_WARN threshold that emits a warning without failing
Oversized diffs should also trigger auto-decomposition if SPIRAL_DECOMPOSE_THRESHOLD is set (see related story)

userStories.title: Add SPIRAL_GIT_AUTHOR env to tag AI-generated commits with a distinct git identity
userStories.description: Commits produced by SPIRAL inherit the machine's default git config identity, making it impossible to distinguish human commits from AI-generated ones in `git log`. Setting SPIRAL_GIT_AUTHOR and SPIRAL_GIT_EMAIL in spiral.config.sh (e.g., 'SPIRAL Agent <spiral@noreply.local>') enables clear attribution, facilitates audit, and allows git blame and tooling to surface which lines were AI-generated.
acceptanceCriteria: SPIRAL_GIT_AUTHOR and SPIRAL_GIT_EMAIL env vars (optional) are read in ralph.sh before git commit
When set, commits use `git -c user.name=... -c user.email=... commit` to avoid modifying global git config
Commit message includes a 'Generated-By: SPIRAL' trailer when SPIRAL_GIT_AUTHOR is set
When unset, behaviour is unchanged (uses machine git config)
spiral.config.sh template includes commented-out examples for the new vars
Unit test verifies the git -c flags are passed correctly in ralph.sh
technicalNotes: Prefer `git -c user.name=... -c user.email=... commit` over GIT_AUTHOR_NAME env var to avoid polluting the child process environment
The 'Generated-By' trailer can be appended with: `git interpret-trailers --trailer 'Generated-By: SPIRAL'`
SPIRAL_VERSION var (pending story) can be included in the trailer: 'Generated-By: SPIRAL/v1.2.3'

userStories.title: Add prd.json story count health check: warn when total exceeds SPIRAL_MAX_STORIES
userStories.description: As SPIRAL runs Phase M across many iterations, prd.json can accumulate hundreds of stories. Very large PRDs degrade AI context quality (Claude must process the full prd.json on each ralph.sh invocation), slow jq operations, and risk exceeding context window limits. A preflight check that warns when total story count exceeds SPIRAL_MAX_STORIES (default 200) prompts operators to archive passing stories before context quality degrades.
acceptanceCriteria: SPIRAL_MAX_STORIES env var (default 200) sets the story count warning threshold
validate_preflight.sh checks total story count and emits a WARNING when count > SPIRAL_MAX_STORIES
SPIRAL_MAX_STORIES_ABORT env var (default 0/disabled) causes preflight to fail hard when count is exceeded
The warning message includes current count, threshold, and a suggestion to archive passing stories
spiral-doctor command (pending story) also reports story count in its health summary
technicalNotes: Count stories with: `jq '.stories | length' prd.json`
Consider a companion `spiral.sh --archive-passed` flag that moves stories with passes:true to prd.archive.json to reduce active PRD size
The 200 default is based on typical Claude context window capacity for structured JSON (~50K tokens for 200 medium-complexity stories at current model limits)

userStories.title: Auto-trigger story decomposition when retry_count reaches SPIRAL_DECOMPOSE_THRESHOLD
userStories.description: Story decomposition currently requires manual intervention or is triggered only on specific failure patterns. Stories that repeatedly fail without triggering existing failure analysis are stuck in an infinite retry loop until MAX_RETRIES marks them _skipped. Adding automatic decomposition at SPIRAL_DECOMPOSE_THRESHOLD (default 2) breaks the loop earlier by splitting the story into subtasks before retries are exhausted.
acceptanceCriteria: SPIRAL_DECOMPOSE_THRESHOLD env var (default 2) sets the retry count at which auto-decomposition triggers
In ralph.sh, after incrementing retry_count, check if retry_count >= SPIRAL_DECOMPOSE_THRESHOLD and invoke decompose_story.py
Auto-decomposition is logged to spiral_events.jsonl as an 'auto_decompose' event with parent story ID and generated child IDs
The parent story is marked _skipped: true with _failureReason: 'auto_decomposed' after successful decomposition
SPIRAL_DECOMPOSE_THRESHOLD=0 disables auto-decomposition (current behaviour)
Unit test verifies the threshold trigger path: story at threshold invokes decompose_story.py
technicalNotes: decompose_story.py already exists; this story wires ralph.sh to call it automatically at threshold
Decomposed child stories should inherit the parent's epicId (pending story) for report grouping
Distinguish from _skipped via MAX_RETRIES — auto-decomposed stories should not count against SPIRAL_COST_CEILING since work continues via children

userStories.title: Add optional Semgrep/Bandit security scan gate after Phase I implementation
userStories.description: Research shows 30-40% of AI-generated code contains security vulnerabilities including SQL injection, path traversal, and weak authentication patterns. Adding an optional Phase S (Security) between Phase I and Phase V, triggered by SPIRAL_SECURITY_SCAN=true, runs Semgrep (or Bandit for Python-only projects) on newly changed files and fails the story on HIGH-severity findings before the test suite runs.
acceptanceCriteria: SPIRAL_SECURITY_SCAN=true env var enables the optional Phase S gate
SPIRAL_SECURITY_SCAN_TOOL env var selects the scanner: 'semgrep' (default) or 'bandit'
Phase S scans only files touched by the current story's commit (not the full codebase)
HIGH-severity findings fail the story with _failureReason: 'security_scan_failure'; MEDIUM findings emit a warning only
Phase S results are written to .spiral/security_scan_{STORY_ID}.json
When the scanner binary is not found, Phase S is skipped with a logged warning (not a failure)
SPIRAL_SECURITY_SCAN_ARGS env var passes additional flags to the scanner
technicalNotes: Semgrep invocation: `semgrep --config=auto --json --output=scan.json $(git diff --name-only HEAD~1)`
Bandit invocation: `bandit -r $(git diff --name-only HEAD~1 | grep '.py$') -f json -o scan.json`
Parse .results[].extra.severity for Semgrep output; .results[].issue_severity for Bandit output
Reference: OpenSSF Security-Focused Guide for AI Code Assistant Instructions; AWS MCP Security Scanner pattern

userStories.title: Add story dependency auto-inference from filesTouch overlap in partition_prd.py
userStories.description: Story dependencies in prd.json are set manually or left empty, causing the partitioner to assign file-conflicting stories to different workers that later conflict on merge. Scanning filesTouch hints populated by populate_hints.py and detecting pairs that share files allows SPIRAL to suggest (or automatically add) dependency edges, improving partition quality and reducing merge conflicts in parallel worker runs.
acceptanceCriteria: New script lib/infer_dependencies.py reads prd.json and computes file-touch overlap between all pending story pairs
Story pairs sharing >= 50% of one story's filesTouch entries are flagged as strongly dependent
Strong dependencies are written to prd.json dependencies arrays when SPIRAL_AUTO_INFER_DEPS=true
Weak overlaps (> 0 but < 50%) are written to .spiral/_dependency_hints.json without modifying prd.json
infer_dependencies.py is called at the end of Phase M so new stories get hints before partitioning
Auto-inferred dependency edges pass check_dag.py cycle detection before being written
Unit tests cover: empty filesTouch, full overlap, partial overlap, and would-create-cycle scenarios
technicalNotes: Jaccard similarity: |A ∩ B| / |A ∪ B| — threshold 0.5 for strong dependency classification
Stories with no filesTouch history (brand new, no prior commits) cannot be inferred and are skipped
This directly addresses the 'new story areas added after baseline have zero hints' gap in partition_prd.py

userStories.title: Add circuit breaker for LLM API calls with half-open recovery
userStories.description: Implement a three-state circuit breaker (CLOSED/OPEN/HALF_OPEN) in ralph.sh for LLM API calls. When the failure count exceeds a threshold, the breaker trips to OPEN and blocks further calls for a cooldown period. After the cooldown, it enters HALF_OPEN and allows a single probe call to test recovery before re-closing.
acceptanceCriteria: Circuit breaker state is persisted in .spiral/circuit_breaker.json with fields: state, failure_count, last_failure_ts, cooldown_secs
After 5 consecutive API failures (429/502/503), state transitions to OPEN and no further LLM calls are made for SPIRAL_CB_COOLDOWN_SECS (default 60)
After cooldown, a single probe call is made; success resets to CLOSED, failure restarts cooldown
SPIRAL_CB_FAILURE_THRESHOLD and SPIRAL_CB_COOLDOWN_SECS are documented in spiral.config.example.sh
A bats test simulates 5 failures and verifies state transitions to OPEN, then HALF_OPEN after cooldown
technicalNotes: State file must be written atomically (write-to-tmp then mv) to avoid corruption during concurrent worker reads
Circuit breaker is per-model-endpoint, not global, to allow fallback routing
Distinguish transient errors (5xx, 429) from permanent errors (400, 401) — only transient errors increment failure count
Log all state transitions as circuit_breaker_open / circuit_breaker_closed events to spiral_events.jsonl

userStories.title: Add per-phase timeout with two-phase kill to all LLM invocations
userStories.description: Wrap every LLM CLI invocation (claude, amp, codex) in ralph.sh and spiral.sh with `timeout --kill-after=30 <phase_limit>` so that a hung API call cannot block a worker indefinitely.
