Plugins receive story context as JSON on stdin and may exit non-zero to abort the triggering operation
spiral --list-plugins prints all loaded plugins with name, version, and declared hooks
A sample plugin (plugins/notify-slack/) ships as documentation of the plugin contract
Malformed plugin.toml emits a warning and skips the plugin without crashing SPIRAL
technicalNotes: Declarative plugin manifest pattern from Claude Code plugins architecture
plugin.toml fields: name (string), version (semver string), hooks (array of hook point names), allowed_env (array of required env var names)
Hook scripts are executed via bash with a 30-second timeout enforced by timeout(1)

userStories.title: Add Slack notification plugin for story lifecycle events
userStories.description: Development teams need real-time visibility into SPIRAL run progress in their chat tool. Add a built-in Slack notification plugin that posts a message to a configured channel when a story passes, fails, or when a full run completes. Uses Slack Incoming Webhooks so no OAuth flow is needed.
acceptanceCriteria: Setting SPIRAL_SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL env var activates Slack notifications automatically
A message is posted when a story transitions to passed or failed, including story title and retry count
A summary message is posted on run completion with total stories passed/failed/skipped and run duration
Messages use Slack Block Kit formatting with colour-coded attachments (green=passed, red=failed)
Notification failures are non-fatal: SPIRAL logs a warning and continues the run
SPIRAL_SLACK_EVENTS env var accepts a comma-separated subset of events (e.g. failed,completed) to reduce noise
technicalNotes: Slack Incoming Webhooks API: https://api.slack.com/messaging/webhooks
Block Kit reference: https://api.slack.com/reference/block-kit/blocks
Implement as a plugin in plugins/notify-slack/ using the plugin system from the companion story
dependencies: Implement SPIRAL plugin system with hook-based extension points

userStories.title: Generate Mermaid dependency graph of prd.json stories on demand
userStories.description: prd.json stories have a dependencies array but there is no visual way to inspect the graph before running SPIRAL. Add a spiral graph command that reads dependencies and generates a Mermaid flowchart (LR direction) showing story IDs, titles, and dependency edges, written to docs/dependency-graph.md. This helps authors spot cycles and understand execution order.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral graph outputs a valid Mermaid flowchart block to stdout by default
spiral graph --output docs/dependency-graph.md writes to file and prints the path
Nodes are labelled with story ID and truncated title (max 40 chars)
Stories with no dependencies are visually distinct (different shape or style)
Cycles in the dependency graph are detected, reported as an error, and the offending edges are highlighted in the output
Graph renders correctly in GitHub markdown preview
technicalNotes: Mermaid flowchart syntax: https://mermaid.js.org/syntax/flowchart.html
Use Python networkx for cycle detection (nx.find_cycle) then render to Mermaid string
spiral-ui could embed the Mermaid diagram using mermaid.js CDN in the existing React dashboard

userStories.title: Add `spiral import-jira` command using Jira REST API v3
userStories.description: Enterprises tracking work in Jira Cloud need to feed epics and stories into SPIRAL without manual JSON editing. Add a spiral import-jira command that queries a Jira project via REST API v3 with JQL, maps issue fields to prd.json format, and appends stories with state=pending. Jira priority levels map to SPIRAL priority strings and the Jira issue key is stored for two-way traceability.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral import-jira --host mycompany.atlassian.net --project ENG --jql 'labels=spiral AND status=Backlog' fetches matching issues
Jira priority Highest/High maps to critical/high; Medium maps to medium; Low/Lowest maps to low
Jira issue key (e.g. ENG-42) is stored in story metadata under jiraKey field
Authentication uses JIRA_API_TOKEN + JIRA_USER_EMAIL env vars with Basic auth base64 encoding
Pagination handles >50 results using Jira's maxResults/startAt cursor mechanism
Dry-run flag prints preview without modifying prd.json
technicalNotes: Jira REST API v3 issues search: https://developer.atlassian.com/cloud/jira/platform/rest/v3/api-group-issue-search/
Authentication: base64(email:api_token) passed as Authorization: Basic header
Use requests library; paginate by incrementing startAt by maxResults (default 50) until total is reached

userStories.title: Render interactive story dependency graph in spiral-ui React dashboard
userStories.description: spiral-ui currently shows story cards in a list but does not visualise dependency relationships. Integrate mermaid.js into the React dashboard to render a live dependency flowchart sourced from prd.json dependencies arrays, with colour-coded nodes reflecting current story status. This gives operators an at-a-glance view of the critical path and any blocked stories.
acceptanceCriteria: A new Graph tab in spiral-ui renders a Mermaid LR flowchart of all stories and their dependency edges
Node colours reflect story status: pending=grey, in_progress=blue, passed=green, failed=red, skipped=yellow
Clicking a node opens the story detail panel (existing drilldown component)
The graph re-renders automatically when prd.json is polled for updates (existing polling interval)
The graph renders within 2 seconds for a PRD with 100 stories on a modern browser
technicalNotes: mermaid.js React integration: npm install mermaid and use useEffect to call mermaid.initialize + mermaid.run
Generate Mermaid source string client-side from the prd.json data already fetched by the dashboard
Mermaid npm package: https://mermaid.js.org/
dependencies: Generate Mermaid dependency graph of prd.json stories on demand

userStories.title: Run LLM Guard input scanner on Phase R web-fetched content
userStories.description: Phase R fetches arbitrary web content and documentation which is passed into subsequent LLM prompts, creating an indirect prompt injection surface. Integrate LLM Guard's PromptInjection input scanner to scan fetched research content before it is inserted into prompts, blocking content that scores above a configurable threshold.
acceptanceCriteria: Web-fetched content in Phase R is passed through LLM Guard PromptInjection scanner before prompt assembly
Scan threshold is configurable via SPIRAL_INJECTION_THRESHOLD env var (default 0.8, range 0.0-1.0)
Content exceeding the threshold is truncated and replaced with a sanitized placeholder; Phase R continues with the remaining sources
Scan results (score, threshold, truncated boolean) are recorded in the story's JSONL event log
Performance: scanning a 4KB text block completes in under 500ms on CPU-only hardware
technicalNotes: LLM Guard PromptInjection scanner: https://protectai.github.io/llm-guard/input_scanners/prompt_injection/
pip install llm-guard; from llm_guard.input_scanners import PromptInjection
LLM Guard uses a fine-tuned deberta-v3-base-prompt-injection model; cached locally after first download

userStories.title: Export dependency graph as Graphviz DOT file for advanced rendering
userStories.description: Mermaid covers basic graph needs but large PRDs with 100+ stories render poorly in it. Add a --format dot flag to spiral graph that outputs a Graphviz DOT file with cluster subgraphs grouping stories by priority tier. DOT files can be rendered by graphviz dot CLI or imported into draw.io for publication-quality diagrams.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral graph --format dot produces a valid DOT digraph consumable by graphviz dot -Tsvg
Nodes are coloured by status: pending=white, in_progress=yellow, passed=green, failed=red
Priority clusters group nodes: subgraph cluster_critical, cluster_high, cluster_medium, cluster_low
The output DOT file passes dot -Tsvg without warnings
spiral graph --format dot | dot -Tsvg > graph.svg produces a readable SVG for a 50-story PRD
technicalNotes: Graphviz DOT language reference: https://graphviz.org/doc/info/lang.html
Use Python graphviz package for programmatic DOT generation
Node color attribute uses HTML color names compatible with both dot and neato engines
dependencies: Generate Mermaid dependency graph of prd.json stories on demand

userStories.title: Publish story-level OTel trace IDs to GitHub PR description
userStories.description: SPIRAL creates GitHub PRs per story but the PR description currently contains no link to observability data. When OTel tracing is active and a trace ID is available for a story, append an Observability section to the PR body containing the trace ID and a direct link to the configured OTel UI (Jaeger, Zipkin, or Grafana Tempo). Closes the loop between code review and performance data.
acceptanceCriteria: When SPIRAL_OTLP_UI_URL is set, generated PR descriptions include an Observability section with trace URL
Trace URL is formatted as {SPIRAL_OTLP_UI_URL}/trace/{trace_id}
If no trace ID is available the section is omitted and no placeholder is left
PR description update is idempotent: re-running on an existing PR appends only if section is absent
Integration test mocks the GitHub API and verifies the PR body contains the trace URL
technicalNotes: OTel trace ID is available from the root span context; store it to story metadata after Phase R starts
GitHub PR update uses gh pr edit --body or PATCH /repos/:owner/:repo/pulls/:pull_number
Grafana Tempo trace URL format: {tempo_host}/explore with datasource=Tempo and query=traceId
dependencies: Emit OTel GenAI agent spans per story phase using semconv

userStories.title: Generate per-story ADR stub when architectural decision is detected in Phase I output
userStories.description: SPIRAL's existing ADR generation is triggered globally but cannot detect when a single story makes an architectural decision. Add heuristic detection on Phase I diffs: if a new import/dependency or interface-level function signature change is found, auto-generate an ADR stub in docs/adr/ with the story ID, decision, and alternatives-considered template pre-filled from the story's technicalNotes.
acceptanceCriteria: Phase I post-processing scans the git diff for: new lines in requirements/package files, new function signatures in public module files
When heuristic fires, docs/adr/NNNN-{story-id-slug}.md is created using the MADR template format
ADR stub includes: title, date, status=proposed, context from story description, decision from technicalNotes[0], and a blank Consequences section
ADR creation is recorded as a phase event in the JSONL event log
No ADR is created when no qualifying diff patterns are detected (verified by unit test with a pure-logic diff)
technicalNotes: MADR (Markdown Architectural Decision Records) template: https://adr.github.io/madr/
Heuristic: git diff --name-only | grep -E 'requirements.txt|package.json|go.mod|Cargo.toml' or diff lines matching new function/class/export declarations
ADR numbering: count existing docs/adr/*.md files and increment by 1

userStories.title: Add pip-audit and npm audit dependency vulnerability scan gate in CI
userStories.description: Run pip-audit on Python dependencies and npm audit on Node dependencies as a CI gate that fails the build on HIGH or CRITICAL CVEs. AI-generated code that introduces new dependencies could silently pull in vulnerable packages; automated scanning catches these before merge.
acceptanceCriteria: GitHub Actions workflow runs pip-audit and fails on severity >= HIGH
npm audit --audit-level=high is run when package.json is present
Scan results are uploaded as a CI artifact in JSON format
A known-safe suppress list (audit-ignore.txt or .pip-audit.toml) is supported to handle false positives
technicalNotes: pip-audit docs: https://pypi.org/project/pip-audit/
npm audit: https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v10/commands/npm-audit
Use pip-audit --format json --output pip-audit-results.json
Pin pip-audit version in CI to ensure reproducible scans

userStories.title: Validate LLM JSON outputs with Pydantic models before downstream processing
userStories.description: Parse every LLM-returned JSON blob through a strict Pydantic v2 model before any downstream code processes it. Currently, malformed or unexpected LLM output can cause cryptic KeyError or AttributeError failures deep in the pipeline; Pydantic validation surfaces the exact field mismatch at the point of ingestion with a structured error.
acceptanceCriteria: Pydantic models are defined for all LLM output shapes: story decomposition, research output, route_stories result, and synthesize_tests report
A ValidationError is caught, logged to spiral_events.jsonl with the raw LLM output, and triggers a retry or graceful skip
The validation error message includes the field path and received value
100% of LLM JSON parse sites use model_validate() rather than bare json.loads()
technicalNotes: Use Pydantic v2 model_validate() with strict=False for lenient coercion where safe
Pydantic v2 docs: https://docs.pydantic.dev/latest/concepts/models/
Define a BaseModel subclass per LLM call site in a new spiral/models/ package
Add model_json_schema() output to CI artifact for schema documentation

userStories.title: Cascade skip status through dependency chain when a dependency is _skipped
userStories.description: When a story is marked _skipped: true, automatically propagate that skip to all downstream stories that declare it as a dependency. Currently, blocked stories stay in pending state and waste worker time attempting implementation when their prerequisite will never be satisfied.
acceptanceCriteria: When a story transitions to _skipped, a cascade function traverses prd.json and marks all direct and transitive dependents as _skipped with _failureReason: 'dependency <ID> was skipped'
Cascaded skips are logged to spiral_events.jsonl with event_type: dependency_cascade_skip
The cascade runs after every story skip, not just at startup
A --no-cascade-skip flag in spiral.sh disables this behavior for debugging
technicalNotes: Implement as a BFS/DFS traversal over the dependency graph in a new cascade_skip() function in state_machine.py
Must be idempotent: running cascade multiple times produces the same result
Update prd.json atomically after computing the full cascade set

userStories.title: Add CodeQL security scanning GitHub Actions workflow for AI-generated code
userStories.description: Add a CodeQL analysis workflow that runs on every push and pull request to detect security vulnerabilities (injection flaws, insecure deserialization, path traversal) in AI-generated code. Standard static analysis like Semgrep focuses on patterns; CodeQL performs dataflow analysis that catches subtle injection chains Semgrep misses.
acceptanceCriteria: GitHub Actions workflow runs CodeQL analysis for Python and JavaScript on push/PR
Workflow uses SHA-pinned github/codeql-action version
Results are uploaded to GitHub Code Scanning via upload-sarif action
High-severity findings block PR merge via required status check
Workflow runs only on changed files using path filters to reduce CI time
technicalNotes: CodeQL setup: https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/code-scanning/creating-an-advanced-setup-for-code-scanning
Use actions/checkout@<SHA> and github/codeql-action/init@<SHA> with pinned SHAs
CodeQL queries: use security-and-quality suite for broader coverage

userStories.title: Graceful degradation: fall back to Claude for Phase R when Gemini returns 503
userStories.description: When the Gemini API returns HTTP 503 Service Unavailable during Phase R pre-analysis, automatically retry once then fall back to Claude with a simplified research prompt rather than failing the phase. Gemini outages are common; current behavior blocks the entire iteration until the user manually intervenes.
acceptanceCriteria: On Gemini 503, Phase R retries once after a 10-second backoff, then switches to Claude with SPIRAL_RESEARCH_MODEL as fallback
Fallback to Claude is logged with event_type: gemini_fallback in spiral_events.jsonl
A SPIRAL_GEMINI_FALLBACK_MODEL env var specifies which Claude model to use (default: claude-haiku-4-5-20251001 for cost)
If both Gemini and Claude fallback fail, Phase R exits with a clear error and the iteration is retried up to SPIRAL_RESEARCH_RETRIES times
spiral-doctor reports SPIRAL_GEMINI_FALLBACK_MODEL in its dependency check
technicalNotes: Google AI Studio API error codes: https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/troubleshooting
Distinguish 503 (transient, fall back) from 400 (bad request, do not retry)
Log which model ultimately served the Phase R response in _iteration_summary.json

userStories.title: HMAC-SHA256 signing for SPIRAL_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK outbound payloads
userStories.description: Sign outbound webhook POST bodies with HMAC-SHA256 using a configurable secret key and include the signature in the X-Spiral-Signature-256 header. Receivers can verify payload authenticity and reject tampered or spoofed requests. Without signing, any actor who learns the webhook URL can inject fabricated events.
acceptanceCriteria: When SPIRAL_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK_SECRET is set, every POST request includes an X-Spiral-Signature-256: sha256=<hex> header
The HMAC key is read from SPIRAL_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK_SECRET env var and never logged
Sending without the secret still works (backward-compatible, header omitted)
spiral-doctor reports a warning when SPIRAL_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK is set but SPIRAL_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK_SECRET is not
technicalNotes: Use Python hmac.new(key, body, hashlib.sha256).hexdigest() to generate signature
Follow GitHub's webhook signing convention: https://docs.github.com/en/webhooks/using-webhooks/securing-your-webhooks
Add SPIRAL_NOTIFY_WEBHOOK_SECRET to spiral.config.sh template comments

userStories.title: Add `spiral import-csv` command to bulk-import stories from CSV spreadsheet
userStories.description: Allow users to import multiple stories at once by pointing `spiral import-csv stories.csv` at a spreadsheet. Product managers and stakeholders often manage backlogs in Excel or Google Sheets; supporting CSV import removes the manual JSON editing step and reduces entry errors.
acceptanceCriteria: Command accepts a CSV file path with columns: title, description, priority, estimatedComplexity, acceptanceCriteria (semicolon-separated), technicalNotes (semicolon-separated)
Imported stories are assigned sequential IDs following the existing SPIRAL_STORY_PREFIX convention
Duplicate titles (exact match) are skipped with a warning rather than creating duplicates
A --dry-run flag prints the parsed stories without writing to prd.json
Invalid rows (missing title or priority) are reported with row numbers and skipped
technicalNotes: Use Python csv.DictReader for parsing; support both comma and tab delimiters via --delimiter flag
Reuse the atomic prd.json write pattern (temp-file rename) from merge_stories.py
Validate priority field against allowed enum values before inserting

userStories.title: Lint prd.json at startup: warn on stories missing acceptanceCriteria
userStories.description: At startup, scan prd.json and emit a structured warning for every story that has an empty or missing acceptanceCriteria array. Acceptance criteria are essential for Phase V validation; stories lacking them will pass validation trivially and produce untested implementations.
acceptanceCriteria: startup lint enumerates stories where acceptanceCriteria is absent, null, or an empty array
Each violation is printed as WARN [prd-lint] Story <ID> '<title>' has no acceptanceCriteria
Violations are also written to spiral_events.jsonl with event_type: prd_lint_warning
A SPIRAL_STRICT_AC=true env var causes the lint to be a hard error that aborts startup
Stories with _skipped: true are excluded from the lint check
technicalNotes: Add prd_lint() function to validate_prd.py or a new prd_lint.py module
Run after prd.json is loaded but before worker dispatch
Consider adding a --fix flag that prompts the user to add criteria interactively

userStories.title: Add `spiral export-report` command to generate Markdown story status report
userStories.description: Generate a human-readable Markdown report summarizing all stories, their current status, failure reasons, and passed commit SHAs. This report serves as a release audit trail and can be attached to pull requests or sprint reviews without requiring access to the raw prd.json file.
acceptanceCriteria: Command writes SPIRAL_REPORT_<timestamp>.md with sections: Summary, Passed Stories, Failed/Skipped Stories, Pending Stories
Each story entry shows: ID, title, status, _passedCommit (if passed), _failureReason (if skipped), retry count
Summary section includes: total stories, pass rate %, total API cost estimate (if tracked)
Output path is configurable via --output flag; defaults to current directory
Report is valid CommonMark Markdown renderable by GitHub
technicalNotes: Read directly from prd.json; no external dependencies needed beyond Python stdlib
Use Python's textwrap.dedent and f-strings for template rendering
Add --format json option to emit machine-readable version for CI artifact ingestion

userStories.title: Validate git user.name and user.email config before any AI commit
userStories.description: Before ralph.sh or any script invokes git commit, verify that git config user.name and user.email are set in either the local repo or global config. Commits without author identity silently fail or produce anonymous commits that break git blame and changelog generation downstream.
acceptanceCriteria: Pre-commit check runs git config user.name and git config user.email; aborts with a clear error if either is empty
Error message provides the exact git commands to fix the missing config
Check is part of spiral-doctor output
When SPIRAL_GIT_AUTHOR is set, it is used as a fallback if local/global config is missing
Check runs in spiral.sh preflight, not just in ralph.sh, so it catches the issue before any worker launches
technicalNotes: Use git config --get user.name and check exit code
If SPIRAL_GIT_AUTHOR=Name <email> is set, auto-configure: git config user.name and user.email from it
Reference: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-config

userStories.title: Record per-phase wall-clock timing in _iteration_summary.json
userStories.description: Capture time.perf_counter() start and end timestamps for each pipeline phase (R, T, I, V, G, M) and write them to _iteration_summary.json under a phase_timings map. This data drives performance optimization decisions and helps users identify which phase is the bottleneck in slow iterations.
acceptanceCriteria: _iteration_summary.json includes phase_timings: {R: {start_epoch: float, duration_seconds: float}, ...} for each phase that ran
Timing is measured with time.perf_counter() for monotonic accuracy
Phase timings are also included in the spiral_events.jsonl phase_end events
spiral_dashboard.py displays a per-phase timing bar chart using the iteration summary data
Phases that were skipped (e.g., Phase V when no stories passed) are omitted from phase_timings
technicalNotes: Python time.perf_counter() docs: https://docs.python.org/3/library/time.html#time.perf_counter
Store epoch timestamp alongside duration for absolute time reference: time.time() for epoch, perf_counter for duration
Consider a PhaseTimer context manager: with PhaseTimer('R') as t: ... that auto-writes to summary

userStories.title: Add exponential backoff with jitter for transient filesystem I/O errors on prd.json
userStories.description: Wrap all prd.json read and write operations in a retry loop with exponential backoff and jitter to handle transient OS-level file lock errors (PermissionError, OSError EACCES/ETXTBSY) on Windows. These errors occur when antivirus or file indexers briefly lock the file; current code raises immediately and corrupts state.
acceptanceCriteria: prd.json read and write functions retry up to 5 times on PermissionError or OSError with errno EACCES/ETXTBSY
Backoff follows: base_delay=0.1s * 2^attempt + random.uniform(0, 0.1) seconds
After all retries fail, the full error is logged to spiral_events.jsonl and re-raised
Retry attempts are logged at DEBUG level with attempt number and delay
Windows-specific errno values (EACCES=13, ETXTBSY=26) are handled alongside POSIX equivalents
technicalNotes: Python errno module: https://docs.python.org/3/library/errno.html
Use tenacity library or implement manually with time.sleep and random.uniform
Test with a mock that raises PermissionError on first N calls then succeeds

userStories.title: Use git sparse-checkout in worker worktrees to reduce checkout footprint for large repos
userStories.description: Configure git sparse-checkout in each worker's worktree so only the files listed in the story's filesTouch hint are checked out. In large monorepos, full worktree checkout can take significant time and disk space; sparse checkout reduces both by 60-90% for stories touching a small subset of files.
acceptanceCriteria: When SPIRAL_SPARSE_CHECKOUT=true and filesTouch is non-empty, worker worktrees are created with git sparse-checkout set <files>
If filesTouch is empty, sparse checkout is skipped and full checkout is used
git sparse-checkout reapply is called when the story's filesTouch list is updated
sparse-checkout mode is cone mode for performance
Feature is disabled by default (opt-in via SPIRAL_SPARSE_CHECKOUT=true)
technicalNotes: git sparse-checkout documentation: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-sparse-checkout
Cone mode is significantly faster than non-cone: use git sparse-checkout init --cone
Ensure sparse-checkout does not interfere with git worktree prune operations

userStories.title: Add _humanNotes and _reviewedBy annotation fields to prd.json story schema
userStories.description: Add two optional human-facing annotation fields to the prd.json story schema: _humanNotes (free-text string array for reviewer comments) and _reviewedBy (username/timestamp pairs). These fields are ignored by all automated pipeline stages but preserved through merges, giving humans a structured place to annotate stories without interfering with automation.
acceptanceCriteria: _humanNotes: string[] and _reviewedBy: {by: string, at: string}[] are valid fields in prd.json stories and pass JSON Schema validation
merge_stories.py preserves these fields when merging new research output (no clobber)
spiral export-report includes _humanNotes in the story entry if non-empty
The spiral_dashboard.py story detail view displays _humanNotes and _reviewedBy if present
These fields are stripped from the story context sent to LLM to avoid prompt contamination
technicalNotes: Add to prd.json JSON Schema file (created by the prd-schema story)
In Pydantic model, mark fields as Optional[list] = None to keep backward compatibility
Document in SPIRAL docs that underscore prefix conventionally marks non-automated fields

userStories.title: Add ASCII dependency tree view to `spiral status` subcommand output
userStories.description: Extend the `spiral status` subcommand to include an ASCII tree rendering of story dependencies, similar to the Unix `tree` command. This gives users a visual overview of the dependency DAG without needing the full dashboard, making it easy to spot which stories are blocked by pending dependencies directly in the terminal.
acceptanceCriteria: `spiral status --tree` renders a Unicode/ASCII box-drawing tree of story dependencies
Each node shows: story ID, title (truncated to 50 chars), and status icon (checkmark/X/hourglass/circle with slash)
Root nodes (no dependencies) are shown at the top level; dependents are indented
Cyclic dependencies (if any) are shown with a [CYCLE] marker instead of infinite recursion
Output respects SPIRAL_LOG_LEVEL: --quiet shows only IDs, default shows title + status
technicalNotes: Implement tree rendering in a new cli/tree_view.py module using recursive DFS
Use Unicode box-drawing characters for cross-platform terminal output
Fall back to ASCII +-- `-- | when TERM does not support Unicode

userStories.title: Add SPIRAL_COMMIT_SIGNING env var to enable GPG/SSH signing on AI-generated commits
userStories.description: Add a SPIRAL_COMMIT_SIGNING env var that, when set to gpg or ssh, configures git to sign AI-generated commits using the user's configured GPG key or SSH signing key. Signed commits provide cryptographic proof that commits attributed to the AI agent actually originated from an authorized SPIRAL run, which is important for compliance in regulated environments.
acceptanceCriteria: When SPIRAL_COMMIT_SIGNING=gpg, ralph.sh passes -c commit.gpgSign=true and -c gpg.program=<gpg> to git commit
When SPIRAL_COMMIT_SIGNING=ssh, ralph.sh sets commit.gpgSign=true and gpg.format=ssh
When SPIRAL_COMMIT_SIGNING is unset or empty, signing is not configured (default behavior unchanged)
spiral-doctor verifies that the required signing key (gpg --list-secret-keys or SSH key) is available when SPIRAL_COMMIT_SIGNING is set
A failed signing attempt aborts the commit with a clear error rather than committing unsigned
technicalNotes: Git commit signing docs: https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Git-Tools-Signing-Your-Work
SSH signing requires git >= 2.34: https://calebhearth.com/sign-git-with-ssh
Pass signing config via -c flags rather than modifying .git/config to keep it ephemeral

userStories.title: Emit OTel agentic-system spans for tasks, actions, teams, and artifacts
userStories.description: The 2025 OTel GenAI agentic-systems proposal defines span conventions for tasks (trackable units of work), actions (tool calls, LLM queries, API requests), teams (agent groups), and artifacts (prompts, code, embeddings). Instrumenting Spiral's worker loop with these conventions enables end-to-end trace correlation across the full story lifecycle and makes telemetry compatible with CNCF-standard observability backends.
acceptanceCriteria: Each story execution emits a root 'task' span with gen_ai.task.id and gen_ai.task.status attributes
Each LLM call inside a worker emits a child 'action' span tagged with gen_ai.action.type = 'llm_query'
Tool/bash executions inside workers emit 'action' spans with gen_ai.action.type = 'tool_call'
Spans carry W3C traceparent headers so they stitch into a single distributed trace
A smoke test verifies at least one task span and one action span appear in the OTLP export
technicalNotes: See OTel gen-ai-agent-spans spec: https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/gen-ai/gen-ai-agent-spans/
GitHub issue tracking agentic conventions: https://github.com/open-telemetry/semantic-conventions/issues/2664
Span kind CLIENT for cross-process, INTERNAL for same-process agent invocations

userStories.title: Pin all GitHub Actions to full commit SHA and enforce via policy
userStories.description: Third-party actions pinned to mutable tags (e.g., actions/checkout@v4) are a supply-chain attack vector; a compromised tag can inject malicious code. Pinning to the immutable full 40-character SHA guarantees the exact code that was reviewed is always executed. GitHub's 2025 allowed-actions policy can now enforce SHA pinning org-wide and block unpinned workflows from running.
acceptanceCriteria: All uses of third-party actions in .github/workflows/*.yml are updated to full-length SHA pins with a version comment
A CI job (pinned-actions-check) runs actionlint or a grep-based check that fails if any action ref matches @v[0-9] or @main/master
README documents the process for updating a pinned action to a new release
No first-party actions (actions/checkout, etc.) are exempted without explicit justification
technicalNotes: StepSecurity best practices: https://www.stepsecurity.io/blog/github-actions-security-best-practices
GitHub 2025 SHA-pinning enforcement announcement: https://github.blog/changelog/2025-08-15-github-actions-policy-now-supports-blocking-and-sha-pinning-actions/
Use 'actionlint' linter or 'pin-github-action' CLI tool for bulk pinning

userStories.title: Replace GitHub Actions long-lived secrets with OIDC short-lived tokens
userStories.description: Long-lived API keys stored as GitHub Secrets (e.g., for AWS, GCP, or Anthropic billing) are a persistent credential-leak risk. GitHub Actions OIDC lets cloud providers and services issue short-lived tokens scoped to a single workflow job, automatically expiring after the job ends. This eliminates secret rotation burden and reduces the blast radius of any credential compromise.
acceptanceCriteria: AWS/GCP deploy steps use OIDC role assumption instead of static key secrets where applicable
Workflows that use OIDC have id-token: write in permissions and read-only for all other scopes
Trust policy restricts token issuance to exact repository and branch (no wildcards)
A checklist in .github/SECURITY.md documents which integrations still require static secrets and why
technicalNotes: GitHub OIDC concepts: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/concepts/security/openid-connect
AWS OIDC setup: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/deployment/security-hardening-your-deployments/configuring-openid-connect-in-amazon-web-services
Minimum TTL for OIDC tokens is one workflow job; no explicit revocation needed

userStories.title: Replace shell=True subprocess calls with create_subprocess_exec to prevent injection
userStories.description: Python worker scripts that pass shell=True (or subprocess.run with a string command) are vulnerable to shell injection if any part of the command is derived from LLM output, story titles, or branch names. Migrating to asyncio.create_subprocess_exec() (or subprocess.run with a list of args) executes commands without a shell interpreter, making injection impossible regardless of input content.
acceptanceCriteria: A grep CI check fails if any Python file in the project contains 'shell=True' without a '# spiral-allow-shell' inline comment
All existing shell=True call sites in worker utilities are migrated to exec-form equivalents
Pipe deadlock prevention: all subprocess calls that capture stdout also capture stderr or use communicate()
Tests cover at least one case with a command argument containing shell-special characters (semicolons, backticks)
technicalNotes: asyncio.create_subprocess_exec docs: https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-subprocess.html
Security rationale: https://runebook.dev/en/docs/python/library/asyncio-eventloop/asyncio.loop.subprocess_shell
Use asyncio.wait_for(proc.communicate(), timeout=N) to prevent pipe deadlocks and hangs

userStories.title: Implement explicit prompt cache breakpoints for system prompts and PRD context
userStories.description: Claude's prompt caching charges cache reads at 10% of normal input token cost (90% discount) when cache_control breakpoints are explicitly placed at the end of static content. Spiral re-sends the full system prompt and PRD context on every LLM call; adding cache_control: {type: ephemeral} breakpoints after these static sections will dramatically reduce per-story API costs, especially for large PRDs.
acceptanceCriteria: All LLM call sites in Python workers that send system_prompt + prd_context add a cache_control breakpoint after the last static block
Token cost tracking differentiates cache_read_input_tokens from input_tokens in cost calculations
Cache hit rate is logged per story phase (R/D/I/T) and surfaced in the dashboard
An integration test verifies cache_read_input_tokens > 0 on the second call with the same system prompt
Cost savings from caching are included in the per-story cost report
technicalNotes: Claude prompt caching docs: https://platform.claude.ai/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-caching
Cache TTL is 5 minutes for Claude; batched calls must complete within the TTL window
Place breakpoints after: system prompt, PRD snapshot, story context — before the per-call user message
Gemini context caching requires 32k+ token minimum and explicit cache object creation via API

userStories.title: Propagate W3C trace context across worker subprocess boundaries
userStories.description: When spiral.sh spawns worker subprocesses (bash or Python), the parent trace context is currently lost, creating disconnected traces in any OTLP backend. Injecting the W3C traceparent/tracestate environment variables into each worker's environment and reading them at startup stitches all spans into a single end-to-end trace per story, making cross-worker debugging practical.
acceptanceCriteria: Parent process writes TRACEPARENT and TRACESTATE env vars before fork/exec of each worker
Worker Python code reads these vars and sets them as the active OTel context on startup
An integration test with a local OTLP collector verifies parent-child span relationship
No regression on existing OTel span tests
technicalNotes: W3C Trace Context spec: https://www.w3.org/TR/trace-context/
OTel context propagation overview: https://opentelemetry.io/docs/concepts/context-propagation/
Python opentelemetry-api TraceContextTextMapPropagator can inject/extract from dicts

userStories.title: Add stale git lock-file detection and safe cleanup to spiral.sh
userStories.description: When a worker is killed mid-operation, git may leave behind a stale .git/index.lock or packed-refs.lock file in the worktree. This causes all subsequent git commands in that worktree to fail with 'Another git process seems to be running', blocking story retries indefinitely. A pre-flight check that detects lock files older than a configurable threshold (default 5 min) and removes them — only after confirming no live git process owns them — prevents this class of stuck worker.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral.sh pre-flight phase checks each active worktree for .git/*.lock files
Lock files older than SPIRAL_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MINUTES (default 5) are removed after verifying no live git PID holds them
An event is logged to the Spiral audit log whenever a stale lock is removed
Unit test (bats) covers: no lock present, fresh lock (skipped), stale lock (removed), stale lock with live PID (skipped)
technicalNotes: Common pattern: check mtime > threshold AND 'ps aux | grep git' returns no matching PID
Claude Code has known issue with stale index.lock: https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/issues/11005
Use 'flock' or 'lsof' to verify ownership before deletion on Linux

userStories.title: Migrate CI Python toolchain from pip to uv for 10-100x faster installs
userStories.description: GitHub Actions CI currently installs Python dependencies with pip, which is slow and inconsistent across runs due to resolver differences. Replacing it with uv — a Rust-based drop-in replacement — provides 10-100x faster cold installs via parallel downloads and system-wide caching, plus reproducible builds via uv sync --frozen. This directly reduces CI wait time and flakiness from resolver non-determinism.
acceptanceCriteria: All GitHub Actions Python setup steps use 'pip install astral-sh/setup-uv' then 'uv sync --frozen'
A requirements.lock (or uv.lock) file is committed and kept up to date
CI cold install time for Python deps is measured before and after; at least 3x speedup documented
Local dev docs updated to recommend 'uv sync' instead of 'pip install -r requirements.txt'
uv.lock file is validated in CI (uv lock --check) to catch drift
technicalNotes: uv docs: https://docs.astral.sh/uv/
uv vs pip comparison: https://realpython.com/uv-vs-pip/
Use --frozen flag in CI to enforce exact lockfile; use --locked for local dev

userStories.title: Add dead-letter queue for permanently failed stories with manual replay command
userStories.description: Stories that exhaust all retry attempts currently stay in a terminal 'failed' state with no structured path to recovery. A dead-letter queue (DLQ) pattern moves exhausted stories into a distinct dlq state in prd.json, preventing them from clogging the active queue and making their failure metadata (error type, last attempt timestamp, retry count) queryable. A 'spiral replay --story US-XYZ' command re-enqueues a DLQ story after human review.
acceptanceCriteria: Stories that fail more than SPIRAL_MAX_RETRIES consecutive times transition to 'dlq' state in prd.json
'spiral list --state dlq' shows all dead-lettered stories with failure reason and timestamp
'spiral replay --story <id>' validates the story is in dlq state, resets retry count to 0, and re-enqueues it
DLQ transitions are written to the audit log
Dashboard shows a DLQ badge/count separate from active failures
technicalNotes: DLQ pattern overview: https://dev.to/andreparis/queue-based-exponential-backoff-a-resilient-retry-pattern-for-distributed-systems-37f3
Distinguish transient failures (network, rate-limit) from permanent failures (invalid story, auth error) — only permanent failures go to DLQ immediately
Store last_error_type and last_error_message in the story's DLQ metadata

userStories.title: Add self-consistency hallucination check for LLM-generated acceptance criteria
userStories.description: When Spiral's Phase D (Design) or Phase R (Research) generates acceptance criteria or implementation plans, the LLM may hallucinate API names, file paths, or library versions that do not exist. A self-consistency check that re-prompts the model with the same input using temperature variation and compares outputs flags divergent claims for human review before implementation begins.
acceptanceCriteria: Phase D runs the design prompt twice with temperature 0.0 and 0.7, then compares JSON outputs
Fields that differ between runs are flagged with a 'low_confidence' marker in prd.json
If more than 20% of acceptance criteria fields are low-confidence, the story is paused and a human-review flag is set
The consistency check cost (second LLM call) is tracked separately in token cost accounting
A --skip-consistency-check flag allows disabling for fast-path small stories
technicalNotes: SelfCheckGPT paper and repo: https://github.com/potsawee/selfcheckgpt
For structured outputs, compare field-by-field rather than full string similarity
Consistency threshold should be configurable via SPIRAL_CONSISTENCY_THRESHOLD env var

userStories.title: Enforce least-privilege permissions block on all GitHub Actions workflow jobs
userStories.description: Without an explicit permissions block, GitHub Actions grants read/write access to the GITHUB_TOKEN for all scopes, giving any compromised step broad repository write access. Adding a top-level permissions: read-all (or no permissions) block and granting only the scopes each job actually needs (e.g., contents: write only for release jobs) limits the blast radius of a supply-chain compromise.
acceptanceCriteria: Every .github/workflows/*.yml file has a top-level 'permissions: {}' or 'permissions: read-all' block
Jobs that write to the repository (e.g., release, PR creation) have a job-level permissions block granting only contents: write and pull-requests: write
A CI lint step (actionlint) fails if any workflow is missing a permissions block
No workflow uses the default implicit 'write-all' permission
technicalNotes: GitHub permissions reference: https://graphite.com/guides/github-actions-permissions
actionlint checks for missing permissions blocks: https://github.com/rhysd/actionlint
Best practice: set permissions at workflow level to read-all, then override at job level for write needs

userStories.title: Add asyncio timeout and communicate() deadlock prevention to all worker subprocess calls
userStories.description: Python worker scripts that use proc.wait() or proc.stdout.read() on subprocesses with large output risk deadlocking when the OS pipe buffer fills — the subprocess blocks on write while the parent blocks on wait. Using proc.communicate() (which drains pipes concurrently) wrapped in asyncio.wait_for() with a hard timeout prevents both deadlocks and hung workers that never terminate.
acceptanceCriteria: All asyncio subprocess calls in Python workers use 'await asyncio.wait_for(proc.communicate(), timeout=SPIRAL_SUBPROCESS_TIMEOUT)' instead of proc.wait()
SPIRAL_SUBPROCESS_TIMEOUT defaults to 300s and is configurable via environment variable
TimeoutExpired exceptions are caught and result in a worker 'timeout' failure state (distinct from 'error')
A test spawns a subprocess that writes 10 MB to stdout and verifies no deadlock occurs
Subprocess timeout events are logged with the command prefix and timeout value for debugging
technicalNotes: asyncio subprocess deadlock explanation: https://docs.python.org/3/library/asyncio-subprocess.html
Pattern: output, err = await asyncio.wait_for(proc.communicate(), timeout=300)
After TimeoutExpired, call proc.kill() then await proc.communicate() to drain and avoid zombie

userStories.title: Add worktree health audit command that detects and reports all anomalies
userStories.description: In long-running Spiral sessions, worktrees accumulate inconsistent states: detached HEADs, branches checked out in multiple worktrees (violating git's safety constraint), stale lock files, and worktrees whose branch has been merged/deleted. A 'spiral worktree audit' command that enumerates all worktrees and reports each anomaly type gives operators a single actionable view before starting a new iteration.
acceptanceCriteria: 'spiral worktree audit' exits non-zero and prints a structured report if any anomaly is found
Detected anomaly types: stale locks, detached HEAD, missing branch, duplicate branch checkout, worktree directory missing from disk
Report includes remediation suggestion for each anomaly type (e.g., 'run spiral worktree prune')
A --fix flag auto-resolves safe anomalies (prune missing, remove stale locks) and leaves unsafe ones for human action
Audit output is machine-parseable JSON when --json flag is passed
technicalNotes: git worktree list --porcelain provides machine-readable worktree state
git worktree best practices: https://www.kenmuse.com/blog/using-git-worktrees-for-concurrent-development/
OpenAI Codex uses worktrees for parallel agents: https://developers.openai.com/codex/app/worktrees

userStories.title: Emit gen_ai.usage.* metric counters for token consumption per story phase
userStories.description: The OTel GenAI metrics spec defines gen_ai.client.token.usage (histogram), gen_ai.client.operation.duration, and gen_ai.server.request.duration as standard metric instruments. Emitting these from every LLM call allows aggregating per-phase token burn (Research, Design, Implement, Test) in any OTLP-compatible metrics backend (Prometheus, Datadog, Grafana Cloud) without custom parsing of log lines.
acceptanceCriteria: Every LLM API call records gen_ai.client.token.usage with attributes: gen_ai.operation.name, gen_ai.system, gen_ai.request.model, spiral.story.phase
gen_ai.client.operation.duration histogram is recorded per call in milliseconds
Metrics are exported via the existing OTLP exporter alongside traces
A Prometheus scrape test verifies the metric names and at least one non-zero sample
Dashboard can display token usage by phase using these metrics
technicalNotes: OTel GenAI metrics spec: https://opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/gen-ai/gen-ai-metrics/
Use opentelemetry-sdk Meter.create_histogram() for token counts
Add spiral.story.id and spiral.story.phase as extra attributes beyond the standard semconv set

userStories.title: Add multi-agent failure attribution logging to identify root cause of cascade failures
userStories.description: When multiple Spiral workers run concurrently and one fails, it can cause downstream story failures through shared state (prd.json writes, git ref conflicts, port contention). Currently there is no structured attribution linking a cascade failure back to its root cause worker. Adding structured failure attribution logs — recording which worker ID triggered a cascade, what resource it was contending for, and which downstream stories were affected — makes incident diagnosis tractable.
acceptanceCriteria: Each worker failure event includes: worker_id, story_id, failure_type (local|cascade), root_cause_worker_id (if cascade), contended_resource
Cascade detection: if worker B fails within 30s of worker A's failure and shares a resource, B's failure is tagged as potential cascade
'spiral diagnose --run <run-id>' prints a causal failure chain for the run
Failure attribution data is stored in a structured failures section of the run's checkpoint JSON
A test scenario with intentional worker conflict verifies cascade attribution is recorded
technicalNotes: ICML 2025 multi-agent failure attribution research: https://syncedreview.com/2025/08/14/which-agent-causes-task-failures-and-whenresearchers-from-psu-and-duke-explores-automated-failure-attribution-of-llm-multi-agent-systems/
Track shared resources per worker: worktree path, branch name, prd.json write lock, port allocations
Use monotonic timestamps to establish happens-before relationships between worker events

userStories.title: Add GitHub Actions job summary with per-story results table on each CI run
userStories.description: CI runs currently produce pass/fail status with no structured summary visible in the GitHub UI. Writing a Markdown summary to $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY with a table of story IDs, statuses, token costs, and durations gives reviewers an at-a-glance view of each iteration's output directly on the Actions run page, without needing to parse raw log lines.
acceptanceCriteria: The CI workflow appends a Markdown table to $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY after each spiral run
Table columns: Story ID, Title, Status (pass/fail/skip), Duration (s), Token Cost ($)
A summary footer shows total stories run, total cost, and total duration
The summary step runs even if earlier steps fail (using 'if: always()')
Summary size stays under 1 MiB (enforced by truncating to top 200 stories if needed)
technicalNotes: GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY docs: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/workflows-and-actions/workflow-commands
GitHub Flavored Markdown is supported; tables, bold, and checkboxes all render
Generate summary from prd.json using jq or Python after each spiral iteration

userStories.title: Use jq --stream for prd.json parsing to support files exceeding 10 MB
userStories.description: As Spiral accumulates hundreds of stories with embedded LLM outputs, prd.json can grow past 10 MB. jq's default mode reads the entire file into memory; on constrained CI runners this causes OOM kills. Migrating prd.json queries in shell scripts to jq --stream mode (combined with fromstream/truncate_stream) enables memory-efficient extraction without loading the full document.
acceptanceCriteria: All jq invocations in spiral.sh and ralph.sh that read prd.json are audited; those reading top-level arrays are migrated to --stream mode
A test script generates a synthetic 15 MB prd.json and verifies jq --stream extraction completes under 512 MB RSS
Streaming queries use compact output (-c) to reduce intermediate allocation
A comment in the script explains why --stream is used, for maintainability
technicalNotes: jq streaming tutorial: https://devblog.songkick.com/parsing-ginormous-json-files-via-streaming-be6561ea8671
Pattern: jq -cn --stream 'fromstream(1|truncate_stream(.,1))' prd.json | jq 'select(.id=="US-123")'
jq 1.8.1 is the recommended version in 2026

userStories.title: Add bats-assert and bats-support libraries to existing bats test suite
userStories.description: The existing bats tests use raw bash assertions ([ ... ] with manual error messages), which produce cryptic output on failure. The bats-assert and bats-support helper libraries provide readable assertion functions (assert_output, assert_line, assert_success, refute_output) that emit diff-style failure messages. Adopting them improves test maintainability and makes CI failure output actionable without running locally.
acceptanceCriteria: bats-assert and bats-support are added as git submodules or npm devDependencies in the test directory
All existing bats tests are migrated to use assert_output / assert_success / assert_failure
At least one new test uses assert_line to check specific lines in multi-line output
CI installs bats helper libraries as part of the test setup step
Test failure output in CI shows the expected vs actual diff without additional tooling
technicalNotes: bats-core repo: https://github.com/bats-core/bats-core
bats-assert/bats-support are sub-projects under the bats-core GitHub org
Install via: git submodule add https://github.com/bats-core/bats-assert test/lib/bats-assert

userStories.title: Add per-job GitHub Actions cache for uv/pip deps and pre-commit hooks
userStories.description: CI jobs that reinstall Python dependencies and pre-commit environments from scratch on every run waste 2-5 minutes per run. Adding actions/cache steps keyed on the uv.lock (or requirements.txt) hash for Python deps and on the pre-commit config hash for hook environments eliminates these redundant installs, directly reducing CI cost and wait time.
acceptanceCriteria: A cache step with key '${{ hashFiles("**/uv.lock") }}' wraps Python dependency install in all CI jobs
Pre-commit hook environments are cached with key '${{ hashFiles(".pre-commit-config.yaml") }}'
Cache restore-keys provide fallback to nearest ancestor cache on miss
CI run duration is measured before and after; at least 2 minutes saved on warm runs
Cache invalidation is documented: changing uv.lock or pre-commit config busts the respective cache
technicalNotes: actions/cache v4 is required (v2/v3 deprecated Feb 2025): https://github.com/actions/cache
uv has its own cache dir (~/.cache/uv); key on uv.lock for lockfile-based installs
Pre-commit cache dir: ~/.cache/pre-commit; separate cache key from Python deps

userStories.title: Structure bats tests to source functions under test for true unit coverage
userStories.description: Current bats tests execute shell scripts as black boxes, meaning individual functions cannot be tested in isolation. Refactoring scripts to guard direct execution with 'if [[ "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" == "${0}" ]]' allows bats tests to source the script and call individual functions, enabling true unit testing of helpers like worktree_prune, cost_check, and lock_cleanup with fine-grained assertions.
acceptanceCriteria: All major shell scripts (spiral.sh, ralph.sh, and key utility scripts) are refactored with BASH_SOURCE guard around their main execution block
At least 5 internal helper functions have dedicated bats unit tests that source the script and call the function directly
Existing integration-style bats tests continue to pass unchanged
A new 'unit' test suite target runs only the function-level tests (fast, no side effects)
CI runs both unit and integration bats suites and reports results separately
technicalNotes: BASH_SOURCE guard pattern: https://www.code-sage.com/blog/devops/2024/10/26/Automated-Testing-for-BASH-with-BATS.html
Move main logic to run_main() function, guard with: if [[ "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" == "${0}" ]]; then run_main "$@"; fi
Use bats-mock (https://github.com/grayhemp/bats-mock) to stub external commands like git, curl, jq

userStories.title: Implement idempotent tool execution with per-story undo stack
userStories.description: Each phase action in ralph.sh should be idempotent so re-running after a partial failure produces the same result. An undo stack records reversible operations (file writes, git commits) before executing them, so a failed story can cleanly roll back to a known-good state instead of leaving partially applied changes.
acceptanceCriteria: ralph.sh records each destructive operation (file write, git commit, branch create) to a per-story undo log before executing it
A 'spiral undo STORY_ID' command replays the undo log in reverse, restoring the worktree to pre-attempt state
Re-running a previously failed story does not double-apply file changes or create duplicate commits
Undo log is stored in the worktree at .spiral/undo/STORY_ID.jsonl and cleaned up on successful merge
A bats test verifies that a simulated mid-story failure leaves the worktree cleanly restorable via the undo log
technicalNotes: Pattern: 'agent undo stacks and transaction components that encapsulate complex logic into atomic, reversible units' (InsightFinder, 2025)
Use git stash or patch files as the undo payload for file-level changes; record git SHA before commit for git-level undo
Undo log format: JSONL with {operation, target, inverse_command, timestamp}

userStories.title: Add flaky bats test detection and quarantine registry
userStories.description: Bats tests that fail intermittently block the Gate phase without distinction from real regressions. A quarantine registry tracks per-test pass/fail history across runs; tests exceeding a flakiness threshold are automatically quarantined so they no longer block the gate while remaining visible for monitoring and eventual fix.
acceptanceCriteria: A JSON registry (.spiral/flaky-tests.json) records per-test pass/fail counts across the last N gate runs (default N=20)
Tests with a flakiness ratio above SPIRAL_FLAKY_THRESHOLD (default 0.3) are marked quarantined
Quarantined tests still execute but their failure does not cause the Gate phase to fail
A 'spiral flaky-tests report' command prints all quarantined tests with their current failure rates
Quarantine is automatically lifted when a test achieves 10 consecutive passes
technicalNotes: Detection approach: retry-based (re-run failing test in same invocation) combined with historical scoring across runs (Atlassian Flakinator pattern, 2025)
Storage: .spiral/flaky-tests.json keyed by test name with {passes, failures, quarantined, lastSeen} fields
Threshold and window size configurable via SPIRAL_FLAKY_THRESHOLD and SPIRAL_FLAKY_WINDOW env vars

userStories.title: Implement observation masking for ralph.sh context window management
userStories.description: As worker loops accumulate large conversation histories, LLM context costs escalate and performance degrades. Observation masking replaces older phase outputs with compact placeholders while retaining the most recent N turns in full, reducing token usage by up to 52% without sacrificing task performance.
acceptanceCriteria: ralph.sh tracks a rolling window of the last 10 phase outputs passed to the LLM (configurable via SPIRAL_CONTEXT_WINDOW)
Phase outputs older than the window are replaced with a one-line summary placeholder before being sent to the LLM
The LLM is informed of masking via a system prompt note: 'earlier phase outputs omitted for brevity'
Token count before and after masking is logged to the iteration summary under _contextStats
Masking achieves at least 40% token reduction on stories with more than 5 completed phases
technicalNotes: JetBrains Research (2025): keeping latest 10 turns gave best balance; masking achieved 52% cost reduction vs full history
Do NOT summarize old turns (adds 7% cost overhead and 13-15% longer runs per JetBrains study); replace with single-line placeholder
Implement in ralph.sh as a bash function that truncates the context array before each LLM call

userStories.title: Add policy adherence validation gate before high-risk phase actions
userStories.description: Research shows agents can achieve perfect tool sequencing while violating safety policies. Before ralph.sh executes high-risk actions (git push, merge, file deletion), a lightweight policy-lookup step checks the action against a configurable allow-list and logs a policy verdict, creating an explicit safety layer separate from LLM instruction following.
acceptanceCriteria: A .spiral/policy.json file defines allowed and forbidden operations per phase (R, I, V, M)
ralph.sh checks the policy file before executing any destructive operation; forbidden operations are logged and skipped
Policy violations are reported in the checkpoint with a dedicated _policyViolations array field
A bats test verifies that a worker attempting a forbidden operation has it blocked and recorded
Policy file ships with safe defaults that can be overridden per project
technicalNotes: Four-pillar framework finding: 'policy non-compliance before executing actions' is a distinct and common agent failure mode (arxiv 2512.12791, 2025)
Keep policy checks in bash (not LLM) to make enforcement deterministic and auditable
Policy file format: {phase: {allow: [patterns], deny: [patterns]}}

userStories.title: Add permission-scoped command allow-list for worker terminal commands
userStories.description: Workers currently have unrestricted shell access within their worktree, allowing accidental or LLM-hallucinated destructive commands. A configurable allow-list of permitted commands per phase prevents unauthorized operations without requiring human approval for routine work.
acceptanceCriteria: A .spiral/command-allowlist.json file maps each phase (R, I, V, M) to a list of permitted command prefixes
ralph.sh intercepts shell commands extracted from LLM output and checks them against the phase allow-list before execution
Commands not on the allow-list are logged as blocked and the LLM is informed with a structured error message
The allow-list ships with safe defaults: Phase R (curl, python, cat, ls, jq), Phase I (file ops, git add/commit), Phase V (bats, pytest), Phase M (git merge)
A bats test verifies a blocked command is not executed and is logged to .spiral/security-events.log
technicalNotes: From martinfowler.com: 'a carefully curated allow-list of terminal commands' is a concrete safety pattern for autonomous code generation agents
Block dangerous patterns by default: 'git push --force', 'rm -rf /', 'git reset --hard' unless explicitly allow-listed
Log blocked commands to .spiral/security-events.log with timestamp, workerId, and full command attempted

userStories.title: Enforce diagnosis-before-action block in Phase I before code modification
userStories.description: A key finding from agentic reliability research is that agents skip diagnostic steps before remediation, leading to incorrect fixes. Phase I workers should be required to output a structured diagnosis block before any code modification, making reasoning explicit and verifiable.
acceptanceCriteria: The Phase I system prompt requires workers to output a diagnosis block before any file edits: {currentState, problemIdentified, plannedChanges}
ralph.sh validates the presence of a diagnosis block in Phase I output before proceeding to execute changes
Missing diagnosis block causes Phase I to re-prompt: 'Diagnosis block required before making changes. Please output your diagnosis first.'
Diagnosis blocks are stored in the checkpoint under _phaseI.diagnosisBlock
A bats test verifies that Phase I output without a diagnosis block triggers re-prompt and does not execute changes
technicalNotes: Tools pillar finding: 'skipped diagnostic steps before remediation' is the highest-frequency tool failure pattern in complex agent scenarios (arxiv 2512.12791)
Pattern: 'enforce diagnostic-before-action patterns; implement DAG-based task dependencies'
Diagnosis block format: require specific section headers (## Current State, ## Problem, ## Planned Changes) for reliable parsing

userStories.title: Launch workers in dedicated process groups for targeted crash cleanup
userStories.description: When a worker crashes, spiral.sh currently risks killing sibling workers by targeting all background jobs. Launching each worker with setsid to create its own process group enables surgical kill-by-PGID, ensuring only the crashed worker and its descendants are cleaned up.
acceptanceCriteria: Each worker is launched with setsid so it leads its own process group
On worker crash detection, spiral.sh kills only the crashed worker's process group: kill -- -PGID
A worker crash is detected within 5 seconds and logged to progress.txt without interrupting running sibling workers
After a worker crash, its worktree is inspected for corrupted shared resources and cleaned before the next retry
A bats test simulates a worker crash (SIGKILL to a worker PID) and verifies sibling workers continue and crash is logged
technicalNotes: Vellum 2025: 'faults in one agent don't cascade to others; specialized agents with limited scope reduce complexity'
QCon SF 2025: 'timeouts and circuit breakers for each parallel branch prevent one slow/failing agent from blocking the entire process'
Use setsid (POSIX) rather than cgroups for portability across Linux and macOS environments

userStories.title: Implement shared-fetch optimization for parallel worker git worktrees
userStories.description: Each worker currently fetches from origin independently, creating redundant network transfers proportional to the number of parallel workers. Since all worktrees share one object database, a single coordinated fetch in the main repo satisfies all workers simultaneously, reducing total fetch time from O(N*12s) to O(1.3s) for 10 workers.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral.sh performs a single 'git fetch origin' in the main repo before launching workers
Workers do not independently fetch; they use 'git reset --hard origin/main' against already-fetched objects
A timing log entry records pre-launch fetch duration vs. estimated per-worker fetch total
With 5+ parallel workers, total fetch time is reduced by at least 50% compared to per-worker fetches
A bats test verifies that only one network fetch event occurs in the git trace log when multiple workers start
technicalNotes: Shared object database: 'a single fetch operation in any worktree synchronizes objects for all worktrees, eliminating redundant network transfers' (gitcheatsheet.dev)
Benchmark reported: 12s sequential per-worktree fetch → 1.3s shared fetch across 10 worktrees
CPU-bound git ops can be parallelized; network ops should be limited to a single shared fetch

userStories.title: Add git diff-index fast-path before full worktree status checks
userStories.description: spiral.sh runs full git status on all worktrees at startup and during gate checks, costing ~80ms per worktree. Replacing this with a lightweight git diff-index --quiet HEAD check (~15ms) as a pre-screen reduces total status check time by up to 81% when most worktrees are clean.
acceptanceCriteria: The worktree status check function uses 'git diff-index --quiet HEAD' as a fast pre-check before running full 'git status'
Full status is only run on worktrees where diff-index reports uncommitted changes
The optimization is logged: 'Skipped full status on N/M worktrees (clean)'
With 10 worktrees and 2 dirty, total check time is under 200ms (benchmark baseline: ~800ms)
A bats test verifies the fast path is taken for clean worktrees and full status for dirty ones
technicalNotes: From gitcheatsheet.dev: selective approach reduces total time from ~800ms to ~150ms for 10 worktrees when only 2 contain changes (81% reduction)
diff-index ~15ms vs. full status ~80ms per worktree
Apply same fast-path pattern to the dirty-worktree detection logic at startup

userStories.title: Implement LLM-as-Judge continuous quality evaluation for phase outputs
userStories.description: Beyond pass/fail metrics, phase outputs should be evaluated for semantic quality. After each Phase R and Phase I completion, a cheap LLM-as-Judge call scores the output for completeness, accuracy, and relevance, storing scores in the checkpoint for trend analysis and early detection of model degradation.
acceptanceCriteria: After Phase R completes, ralph.sh invokes an LLM-as-Judge prompt scoring the research output on a 1-5 scale for relevance and completeness
After Phase I completes, the judge scores implementation quality: follows acceptance criteria, no obvious errors
Scores are stored in the checkpoint under _qualityScores.{phase}: {score, rationale, timestamp}
The dashboard displays average quality scores per phase across the current iteration
Low scores (below SPIRAL_QUALITY_THRESHOLD) emit a warning but do not block progression
technicalNotes: LLM-as-Judge: 'minimal overhead ($0.06, 14.7s)' vs Agent-as-Judge '$0.96, 913s'; use LLM-Judge for continuous monitoring (arxiv 2512.12791)
Use a fast/cheap model (haiku or flash) for judging to minimize cost
Quality scores feed into the production failure telemetry feedback loop

userStories.title: Add tool parameter semantic validation before LLM-directed tool calls
userStories.description: Agent tool failures are the most common failure mode in complex scenarios, often caused by incorrect parameter mapping. Before executing tool calls in ralph.sh, a validation layer checks parameter types and semantics against a tool schema registry, surfacing parameter errors before they cause hard-to-debug downstream failures.
acceptanceCriteria: A .spiral/tool-schema.json file defines expected parameter types for the top 10 tools invoked by workers (git, python, bats, jq, curl)
ralph.sh validates extracted tool call parameters against the schema before execution
Invalid parameters produce a structured error logged to checkpoint under _toolErrors: {tool, parameter, expected, received, timestamp}
A bats test verifies that a malformed tool call is caught at the validation layer and not executed
Schema validation adds no more than 50ms overhead per tool call
technicalNotes: Tools pillar finding: 'incorrect parameter mapping (region name vs. instance ID)' is the most common tool failure mode (arxiv 2512.12791)
Keep validation in bash/python (deterministic) not LLM to avoid recursive validation cost
Start with top 5 tools: git subcommand validation, python module paths, bats test file existence checks

userStories.title: Implement retrieval completeness check before memory-dependent decisions
userStories.description: When workers query prd.json or checkpoint state to make decisions, incomplete retrieval can cause the agent to act on stale or partial information. A completeness check verifies retrieved context satisfies minimum coverage requirements before the LLM proceeds, catching truncated reads and missing fields early.
acceptanceCriteria: Before Phase I begins, ralph.sh verifies the research output contains content for all acceptanceCriteria fields of the story
Before resuming from checkpoint, spiral.sh verifies the checkpoint contains all required state fields (phase, storyId, retryCount)
Incomplete retrieval triggers a re-fetch/re-read with an error logged to progress.txt
After 3 failed completeness checks for the same context, the story is marked blocked with reason 'incomplete_context'
A bats test verifies a truncated checkpoint triggers re-read before resuming
technicalNotes: Memory pillar failure: 'low recall despite high precision (13.1% recall, 30% precision)' — agents act on incomplete context (arxiv 2512.12791)
Mitigation: 'add retrieval coverage checks before decisions'
Coverage check: for prd.json story fields, verify title + description + acceptanceCriteria all non-empty and non-null

userStories.title: Extend spiral.sh --replay with --from-phase and --hint for targeted recovery
userStories.description: The existing --replay flag re-runs a story from scratch. Adding --from-phase and --hint parameters enables targeted recovery: re-execute only from a specific phase using additional operator-supplied context, reusing prior checkpoint state to skip already-completed phases and avoid redundant LLM API calls.
acceptanceCriteria: spiral.sh --replay STORY_ID --from-phase PHASE_LETTER re-executes a story starting from the specified phase
The replay reuses the existing worktree and checkpoint state, skipping phases already marked complete before PHASE_LETTER
An optional --hint 'text' flag injects extra context into the Phase I system prompt for the replay run
Replay attempts are tracked in the checkpoint under _replayHistory: [{timestamp, fromPhase, hint}]
A bats test verifies replay starts from the correct phase and does not re-execute completed phases
technicalNotes: From Vellum guide: 'track and replay tasks with new instructions to understand and improve agent behavior'
Phase letter validation: reject invalid letters and print usage before attempting replay
Hint injection: prepend hint as a system prompt addendum, not user turn, to avoid prompt injection risk

userStories.title: Add production failure telemetry feedback loop for prompt improvement
userStories.description: Failed stories and low-quality phase outputs contain signal for improving system prompts and hints. After each iteration, a script extracts failure patterns from checkpoint data and appends structured feedback to a prompt-improvements log, creating a closed-loop system where production failures drive prompt iteration.
acceptanceCriteria: After each iteration completes, analyze_failures.py scans checkpoints of failed stories and extracts common failure patterns (tool errors, policy violations, low quality scores, context incomplete)
Patterns are written to .spiral/prompt-improvements.jsonl: {storyId, phase, failureCategory, suggestedHint, timestamp}
The dashboard displays a 'Top failure patterns this iteration' section sourced from this log
At least 4 distinct failure categories are recognized: tool_error, policy_violation, context_incomplete, quality_low
A bats test verifies analyze_failures.py correctly categorizes a set of fixture checkpoint files
technicalNotes: InsightFinder pattern: 'production telemetry as the richest input stream for improvement, converting production evidence into training data'
analyze_failures.py is read-only — it must not modify prd.json or checkpoint files
Run analyze_failures.py automatically at the end of each iteration, after Gate phase completes

userStories.title: Emit structured agent telemetry for worker phase transitions
userStories.description: Worker checkpoint files are currently opaque blobs read only on failure. Exposing phase transitions as structured telemetry enables real-time observability of worker health and lets the dashboard surface agent decision points, not just final pass/fail outcomes.
acceptanceCriteria: ralph.sh emits a structured telemetry event to .spiral/agent-telemetry.jsonl after each phase transition: {workerId, storyId, fromPhase, toPhase, durationMs, qualityScore, retryCount, timestamp}
spiral_dashboard.py reads agent-telemetry.jsonl and displays a per-worker phase timeline view
The telemetry log is append-only and rotated at 10MB
A bats test verifies that completing Phase R emits a correctly structured telemetry event
Telemetry is queryable by storyId to reconstruct the full execution timeline for any story
technicalNotes: OTel blog 2025: 'agent memory and state must become first-class citizens; without observing state, you cannot understand decisions'
Distinct from the existing spiral_events.jsonl (generic events): agent-telemetry.jsonl focuses on phase-transition state with durationMs and qualityScore
Wide Events pattern: semi-structured, high-dimensional telemetry reflecting agent semantic state per transition

userStories.title: Implement hierarchical summarization for oversized Phase R research outputs
userStories.description: Phase R research outputs from Gemini can be very long (10,000+ tokens), consuming large portions of the Phase I context window. When research output exceeds a configurable token threshold, a hierarchical summarization step compresses it while preserving all acceptance criteria, technical notes, and source references.
acceptanceCriteria: After Phase R, if research output exceeds SPIRAL_RESEARCH_SUMMARY_THRESHOLD tokens (default 4000), a summarization pass is triggered
The summarizer preserves all distinct technical requirements, source URLs, and acceptance criteria from the original
The summarized output is stored in checkpoint under _phaseR.summary; the full output under _phaseR.full
Phase I receives the summary by default; full output is available via SPIRAL_USE_FULL_RESEARCH=1
A bats test verifies a fixture research output over threshold is summarized and all acceptance criteria appear in the summary
technicalNotes: JetBrains Research 2025: 'hierarchical summarization compresses older conversation segments while preserving essential information'
Target: maintain 95%+ information retention while achieving 3x+ token reduction (LLMLingua-2 benchmark target)
Use a cheap/fast model for summarization (not the main implementation model) to keep cost negligible

userStories.title: Implement human-in-the-loop approval gate for stories marked sensitive
userStories.description: Some stories touch sensitive areas (database migrations, auth, public APIs) where autonomous execution without human review is unacceptable. A 'requiresApproval' flag pauses the worker after Phase I and signals for human approval before Phase V begins, enabling selective oversight without blocking all stories.
acceptanceCriteria: prd.json stories support a boolean 'requiresApproval' field
When a story with requiresApproval:true completes Phase I, the worker writes .spiral/pending-approvals/STORY_ID.json and pauses
A 'spiral approve STORY_ID' command creates .spiral/approvals/STORY_ID.approved to unblock the worker
Pending approvals appear prominently in the dashboard with elapsed wait time
If no approval is received within SPIRAL_APPROVAL_TIMEOUT (default 24h), the story is marked timed_out_awaiting_approval
technicalNotes: Vellum 2025: 'set checkpoints to wait for human approval before continuing' — approval gates for critical workflow junctures
martinfowler.com: 'human in the loop to supervise generation remains essential' for production code changes in sensitive areas
File-based signaling avoids requiring a running web server; poll interval configurable via SPIRAL_APPROVAL_POLL_INTERVAL

userStories.title: Add living PRD changelog tracking story field mutations with author and reason
userStories.description: prd.json is modified by multiple scripts with no audit trail. A changelog section tracks every mutation (status change, field edit, new story added) with timestamp, author script, and reason, enabling teams to understand how the backlog evolved and debug unexpected state transitions.
acceptanceCriteria: prd.json gains a top-level '_changelog' array where each entry has {timestamp, storyId, field, oldValue, newValue, author, reason}
All Python scripts that write to prd.json append a changelog entry for every field they modify
The changelog is append-only; only 'spiral changelog clear' may truncate it
A 'spiral changelog STORY_ID' command prints the full mutation history for a specific story
A bats test verifies that a status change from 'pending' to 'in_progress' generates a correctly structured changelog entry
technicalNotes: Modern PRD best practice 2025: 'utilize a robust system to manage document versions and track changes; avoid silent revisions that create confusion' (Atlassian/Aha.io)
Changelog differs from prd.json backup (already covered): it tracks field-level diffs, not file snapshots
Keep changelog entries compact: max 500 chars for reason field; truncate oldValue/newValue for array fields

userStories.title: Tune parallel worker concurrency by operation type (CPU vs network vs disk)
userStories.description: SPIRAL launches all workers with the same parallelism setting regardless of operation type, causing I/O thrashing on disk-intensive phases. Tuning concurrency by operation type prevents saturation and improves overall throughput by matching parallelism to the bottleneck resource.
acceptanceCriteria: Three separate env vars replace a single SPIRAL_WORKER_COUNT: SPIRAL_CPU_WORKERS (default: nproc), SPIRAL_NET_WORKERS (default: 4), SPIRAL_DISK_WORKERS (default: 2)
spiral.sh selects the appropriate limit by phase: Phase R (network, SPIRAL_NET_WORKERS), Phase I/V (CPU, SPIRAL_CPU_WORKERS), Phase M (disk, SPIRAL_DISK_WORKERS)
The active concurrency limit is logged at phase start: 'Launching N workers (network phase cap)'
A bats test verifies no more than SPIRAL_NET_WORKERS workers run concurrently during Phase R
Backward compatibility: setting SPIRAL_WORKER_COUNT still applies as a cap across all three limits
technicalNotes: gitcheatsheet.dev: 'CPU-bound: match parallelism to core count; Network: limit to 2-4 workers regardless of cores; Disk-bound: restrict to 1-2 workers to prevent I/O thrashing'
Benchmark: four parallel workers reduced test execution from 12 minutes to 5 minutes (58% reduction)
Phase classification: R=network, I=CPU+disk, V=CPU, M=disk

userStories.title: Add dashboard story changelog drilldown view from prd.json _changelog
userStories.description: Reconstruct the full lifecycle of any story in the dashboard from _changelog entries, showing every status change, field edit, and retry with timestamps and the responsible script. This enables operators to audit why a story ended up in its current state without digging through raw JSON.
acceptanceCriteria: The dashboard's per-story drilldown page includes a 'Story Changelog' tab sourced from prd.json._changelog filtered to that storyId
Each changelog entry is rendered as a timeline row: timestamp, changed field, old→new value, author script
The timeline is sorted chronologically and supports filtering by field name
A 'copy as markdown' button exports the story changelog as a markdown table
The changelog tab loads within 500ms even for stories with 100+ changelog entries
technicalNotes: Depends on the 'Add living PRD changelog tracking story field mutations' story for the _changelog field
Render using the existing HTML details element pattern already in the dashboard codebase
Filter implementation: client-side JavaScript, no additional server endpoints required
dependencies: Add living PRD changelog tracking story field mutations with author and reason
